36789.ai_summary: Anna Latta Owens, College Park's first and only female mayor, was a dedicated public servant who fought against a proposed garbage incinerator and established strong ties with the University of Maryland. She was involved in various community initiatives, including the establishment of a pre-school and her work with the Housing Authority Board, and her door was open to all City Councilmembers seeking her insight. Anna Latta Owens will be remembered fondly by those who knew her for her contributions to College Park governance and her improvement of many lives.

36796.ai_summary: The City of College Park has introduced a new composting program, making it easier for residents to recycle food waste with inexpensive compost containers and free weekly collections. As noted, 'composting is one of the easiest and most productive things individuals can do for the environment' and can help combat climate change by reducing the amount of methane produced in landfills. According to a recent study by Prince George's County officials, over a third of the food that ends up in the county landfill could have been composted instead.

36528.ai_summary: The College Park City Council unanimously approved a new district map, the October Plan 1 map, during their meeting on Nov. 15. Councilmember Susan Whitney noted that the alternative Plan 3A map 'would have unevenly distributed the city’s civic associations' and Councilmember Maria Mackie said 'One of the reasons that Autoville and Cherry Hill will no longer be in District 4 is because they’re the means to allowing District 1 to come down into [areas with student housing]. I think that’s very important that people understand that.' The new map will be in effect prior to the November 2023 city election.

key_people: Tyrone Wells, Operations Manager of the Prince George’s County Office of Emergency Management
organizations: Prince George’s County Office of Emergency Management
36357.ai_summary: The College Park City Council discussed emergency management with Tyrone Wells, Operations Manager of the Prince George’s County Office of Emergency Management, who presented the recently updated County Emergency Operations Plan. Councilmembers expressed concerns about the effectiveness of the county's communication and notification systems, with Councilmember John Rigg stating 'Citizens in my community are wondering why they don’t hear from the county more often.' Wells noted that 'We do the best we can to push out information following events,' and recommended alternative sources for alerts and information, such as local news media or dedicated weather radios, highlighting the need for improved emergency preparedness and communication in College Park.

key_people: Gale Mamatova, Vice President of the North College Park Community Association
36359.ai_summary: The City of College Park is undergoing a redistricting process, which has been a heated discussion among residents. Gale Mamatova, Vice President of the North College Park Community Association, suggests that the city needs to revisit its approach to bringing residents along through access to information and effective communication. The city can learn from neighboring cities, such as investing in a community engagement tool and hiring multilingual staff to improve inclusivity, and city leaders need to 'invest in learning to listen with intent so that people around them feel more represented, supported and understood.'

key_people: Kathy Bryant, President of the Old Town Civic Association and District 3 resident
Jordan Dewar, Vice-Chair of the Redistricting Commission
Robert Day, Chair of the Redistricting Commission
36067.ai_summary: The College Park City Council's redistricting debate continued, with residents and councilmembers questioning the Redistricting Commission about their proposals, which would separate core neighborhoods. Kathy Bryant, President of the Old Town Civic Association, expressed concern that the commission's proposals disregard 'long term neighborhood connections that I feel are very important.' Councilmember Fazlul Kabir also emphasized the importance of keeping neighborhoods together, saying 'People don’t really differentiate between the boundaries of these neighborhoods; they all think as one cohesive community.'

36180.ai_summary: College Park Day, the city's signature celebration, is returning on Oct. 15 after a 2-year hiatus. The event will feature about 150 organizations, including food vendors and live entertainment, and is expected to attract 8,000 residents. Councilmember Denise Mitchell recalled that College Park Day grew out of discussions among Councilmember Fazlul Kabir, Mayor Patrick Wojahn, and a small group of residents, and band leader Shawn Sharifi said, 'We can't wait to bring our boogy to College Park Day,' highlighting the event's significance for community engagement and celebration.

key_people: Lisbeth M Melendez Rivera, Ward 2 Election Candidate
35987.ai_summary: Emily Strab has won a tight three-way race to fill the position of city councilmember for Ward 2, receiving 280 votes. The seat was vacant after former Ward 2 councilmember Robert Croslin was elected mayor in a June special mayoral election. Strab will take the oath of office in King Park on Thursday, Oct 20, at 6 p.m.

key_people: Lee Sommer, College Park Airport Manager
David Dorsch, Chair of the College Park Airport Authority
organizations: Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission
key_issues: New commuter program by Tailwind Air
Noise concerns from new air traffic
35792.ai_summary: The College Park City Council discussed a new commuter program by Tailwind Air, which plans to launch four daily flights between College Park Airport and New York City’s Skyport Marina. The program would require changes to the airport's operating regulations, which were last revised in 2018, and has raised concerns about increased noise from new air traffic, with David Dorsch noting that 'the noise is generally the helicopters and the ones that are the worst are the military, because they’re the biggest and heaviest.' The council was due to revisit the issue during their September 13 worksession.

key_people: Christopher Hatcher, attorney for Perseus TDC
35965.ai_summary: A new apartment building, Residences at the Six, is under construction at 6400 America Boulevard in the Prince George’s Plaza Transit District, with 316 units expected to be completed by summer 2024. Councilmember Ben Simasek said the new building 'is a big opportunity to activate University Town Center, to improve the walkability of that area, and generally making it more attractive.' The developer, Perseus TDC, has committed to designating at least 75% of the units as affordable to low- and middle-income households, although there is no city or county requirement to include affordable units.

organizations: City of College Park Department of Public Works
35659.ai_summary: A devastating bow echo storm hit College Park on July 12, causing widespread damage and utility outages, with estimated costs to the city around $750,000. Assistant City Manager Bill Gardiner said the department of public works removed several hundred downed trees from public rights of way, with support from neighboring cities and counties. Residents like Victoria MacDonald and Kathy Bryant were impacted, with MacDonald saying 'Maybe that has to be looked at, in my opinion, what are the criteria?' regarding the use of the University of Maryland's warning siren, and Bryant losing a century-old tree and suffering damage to her home.

key_people: Calvin Hawkins, Prince George’s County Council at-large member
Mel Franklin, Prince George’s County Council at-large member
35657.ai_summary: The July primary election in Prince George’s County saw nearly 214,000 voters turn out, with incumbents Calvin Hawkins and Mel Franklin winning the at-large race. However, some residents, including Jennifer Kubit, reported issues with the voting process, such as being able to choose between Republican and Democratic ballots despite being registered with a party. The College Park City Council discussed staffing issues that stalled the opening of the Ritchie Coliseum polling site on Election Day, inconveniencing voters who had arrived on time.

key_people: James Crane, communications and public safety expert with the Columbia Telecommunications Corporation
Shawn Thompson, vice president for analytics with CTC
Suellen Ferguson, attorney for the city
key_issues: health risks associated with radio-frequency radiation
35636.ai_summary: The College Park City Council met with telecommunications contractors to discuss the planned installation of 5G antennas around the city, with residents expressing concerns about health risks. According to Suellen Ferguson, attorney for the city, a 2018 FCC rule requires that jurisdictions approve eligible Small Wireless Facilities (SWFs) installation requests within 60 days, and the city can only discuss the exact location of proposed arrays, not whether they can be installed at all. Verizon has filed documents to install a 5G array at the intersection of Edgewood Road and Rhode Island Avenue, and will be responsible for more than half of the over 800 proposed telecommunications array sites in Prince George’s County.

key_people: Lisbeth M. Melendez Rivera, City Council Candidate
35721.ai_summary: A special mayoral election was held in Hyattsville, where Robert Croslin won with 1,329 votes. College Park experienced a staffing issue that led to a polling place opening an hour and a half late. Upcoming elections include a special election for the open Ward 2 city council seat in Hyattsville and the November 8 general election, where residents will vote on various measures, including the legalization of recreational marijuana.

35522.ai_summary: Preliminary election results are in for the July 19 primary election in Prince George's County, with many races essentially decided in the Democratic primaries. Wanika Fisher is leading in the Democratic nomination for county councilmember in District 2, while Eric Olson has secured his nomination in District 3. The election results could still change as not all precincts have finished reporting and mail-in and provisional ballots have not yet been counted, which may impact College Park governance and representation in the county council.

key_people: James Rosapepe, State Senator and Vice Chair of the State Senate’s Budget and Taxation Committee
35361.ai_summary: The City of College Park is receiving over $22 million in funding from the State of Maryland for various projects, including $9.7 million for the Route 1 rebuilding project and $5 million for graduate student housing at the University of Maryland. According to State Senator James Rosapepe, the improvements to Route 1 will 'increase safety and create a visually pleasing thoroughfare' and he calls it the 'Champs-Élysées of College Park'. The funding also includes $200,000 for a new playground at the Al-Huda School and $7.9 million to create the Community Preservation Trust to preserve affordable single-family homes.

key_people: Eric Olson, Prince George's County Council Candidate
Sia Finoh, Prince George's County Council Candidate
Eve Shuman, Prince George's County Council Candidate

Jonathan White, Prince George's County Council At-Large Candidate
Angela Alsobrooks, Prince George's County Executive
Leigh Bodden, Prince George's County Executive Candidate
Billy Bridges, Prince George's County Executive Candidate

Elliott Gibson, Prince George's County Sheriff Candidate
Sylvester Jones, Prince George's County Sheriff Candidate

Lee Havis, Maryland State Senate Candidate
35345.ai_summary: The state primary election is set to take place on July 19, with early voting from July 7 to July 14, and many races in the area are decided in the Democratic primaries. College Park residents are paying close attention to candidates in local elections, particularly in District 3, where Eric Olson is running against Sia Finoh and Eve Shuman. The election matters for College Park governance as it will determine the representation of the city in the county council and other local offices.

key_people: Claire Worshtil, Lead Strategic Park Planner
Mary Cook, Former NCPCA President and Former College Park City Councilmember
organizations: Prince George’s County Department of Parks and Recreation
35359.ai_summary: The Prince George’s County Department of Parks and Recreation is planning a new community center in North College Park, with a feasibility study expected to conclude by the end of 2022. As Claire Worshtil, Lead Strategic Park Planner, noted, 'We can create flexible spaces,' and 'Seniors could use a room in the mornings, and teens could use the same room … in the afternoons.' The project has secured $1 million in support from the Maryland state budget, courtesy of State Delegate Ben Barnes, and the City of College Park has also pledged financial support, as the community center is expected to 'serve as a catalyst for bringing the community together and for more interaction between neighbors,' according to Incoming NCPCA President Alaina Pitt.

key_people: Sia Finoh, Former House of Delegate Candidate
Eve Shuman, Former Staffer for U.S. Senator Chris Van Holland
34640.ai_summary: The state primary is set for July 19, with early voting from July 7 to July 14, and many races in the area will be decided in the Democratic primaries. Candidates such as Wanika Fisher, Victor Ramirez, and Eric Olson are running for county council seats, while Angela Alsobrooks is running for re-election as county executive. The elections are significant for College Park governance as they will shape the county's representation and policies, with key issues including redistricting and primary elections.

35179.ai_summary: The College Park City Council passed a law on May 10 to protect the city's big, older trees by requiring permits for tree removal and pruning, and mandating tree replacement. Residents who remove trees without a permit will face fines, initially $500 and increasing to $1,000 starting February 1, 2023. Councilmember Maria Mackie introduced an amendment to reimburse property owners up to $300 for planting new trees, while some residents expressed concerns about the ordinance, with Robert O'Brien noting that 'trees are a community resource' and Mary Cook stating that 'fines can eat paychecks and cause emotional distress'.

key_people: Jeff DeHan, Associate Director of Stormwater Management for Prince George's County Department of the Environment
Seyad Sadat, Lead Consulting Engineer at RK&K Civil Engineering
organizations: Prince George's County Stormwater Management Division
Prince George's County Department of the Environment
Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission
35203.ai_summary: The City of College Park is experiencing delays in the Calvert Hills drainage project, which aims to address the issue of flooding in the area. As Councilmember John Rigg stated, 'During the massive rainstorm of September 2020, there was literally 3 to 4 feet of water in the streets. And some neighbors experience flooded basements or sewer back-ups nearly every year.' The project, estimated to cost $15.5 million, has been plagued by delays and is more than a year behind schedule. Councilmember Stuart Adams noted, 'Unfortunately, with global warming, what were considered 5-year storms when these neighborhoods were built can now happen once or twice a year, and we may see 100-year floods every decade.'

key_people: Mike Hunninghake, Sustainable Maryland Program Manager
organizations: University of Maryland’s Environmental Finance Center
35210.ai_summary: Sustainable Maryland provides support and access to resources for Maryland municipalities to become more sustainable, and has certified 41 municipalities, including College Park. As Mike Hunninghake, Sustainable Maryland’s program manager, stated, 'The program operates on the premise that by engaging local governments in sustainability we build a strong foundation for the work that needs to be done with respect to critical issues like climate change, environmental justice, and strong local economies.' The program has helped College Park establish its own Green Team and implement corresponding sustainability programs, including a five-year strategic plan with goals such as establishing community gardens and maintaining a bikeshare program.

key_people: Carlo Colella, University of Maryland Vice President and Chief Administrative Officer
35042.ai_summary: The College Park city council has approved funding for live-video monitoring capacity in the fiscal 2023 budget, with the University of Maryland Police Department (UMPD) set to monitor 22 cameras in areas with high student activity. According to UMPD Chief David Mitchell, 'One of our major concerns is the increased call for service as well as mental health.' Councilmember Llatera Brown Esters noted that 'Live-monitoring, in addition to police patrols and other safety precautions, can assist with a more proactive response to criminal and safety issues that arise.' The live-monitoring program is expected to enhance crime prevention and community safety in College Park.

key_people: Robert Marsili, City of College Park Director of Public Works
Bill Lescure, descendant of the Calverts
Thomas Taltavull, Principal with TJT Architects
organizations: Maryland National-Capital Park and Planning Commission
35026.ai_summary: The City of College Park has been restoring the historic Old Parish House, a single-story structure built in 1817, with the goal of making it a 'showpiece for the public' according to Robert Marsili, the city's director of public works. The restoration has been ongoing for several years, with the final work underway this spring, and has included replacing the roof and ceilings, repairing and replacing windows, and exterior masonry work. As Marsili noted, 'We worked hard to do this in a historically accurate way' and the city also chose to use a local firm, with a portion of the masonry work paid for with a preservation grant of $38,500 from the Maryland National-Capital Park and Planning Commission.

key_people: Petrella Robinson, Commission Member and Mayor of North Brentwood
Isabella Alcañiz, Associate Professor of Government and Politics and Director of the University of Maryland’s Latin American and Caribbean Studies Center
35037.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has established a Restorative Justice Commission to address the historical wrongs committed against the Lakeland community, which was severely impacted by the city's urban renewal project in the 1970s. Mayor Patrick Wojahn stated that the initiative aims to “make the community whole for what occurred in the past and put them as close as possible into the place or even better than they were before the wrong occurred.” The commission includes members such as Petrella Robinson, who witnessed the effects of urban renewal firsthand, and Isabella Alcañiz, an expert in transitional justice, and will work to restore and preserve the Lakeland community.

key_people: Hayden Renaghan, UMD Student Government Association’s deputy liaison to the College Park City Council
Kislay Parahsar, UMD student body president
Dan Alpert, former SGA liaison to the city council
organizations: College Park Department of Public Works
35015.ai_summary: Hayden Renaghan, the UMD Student Government Association’s deputy liaison to the College Park City Council, is working to improve the city through the Clean City Project, a collaborative initiative that tackles weekly street clean-ups. As Renaghan explained, 'Students want it known that it’s their town, too, not just a pit-stop for them.' Renaghan emphasizes the importance of ongoing engagement, stating 'We really have to encourage the leadership to remain diligent in the initiatives we undertake' and 'Show people why they should care. Prove there are tangible benefits to respecting their community, to contributing, to leaving it a better place than they found it.'

key_people: Mary Cook, President of the North College Park Citizens Association
Claire Worshtil, Lead Strategic Park Planner for M-NCPPC
organizations: Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission (M-NCPPC)
College Park Community Center-Youth Sports Complex
34892.ai_summary: The city of College Park has started a feasibility study for a new community center in the North College Park neighborhood, with the goal of identifying residents' priorities for the center. As Councilmember Fazul Kabir (District 1) stated, 'A partnership will help expedite the construction. This will also help the city to have its stake in the way the services will be provided in the center once it’s built.' The city will likely pay for a portion of the new facility, while the Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission (M-NCPPC) would foot most of the bill, and the study will determine the type of facility that best addresses residents' priorities.

34748.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is focused on encouraging homeownership through new programs, aiming to achieve a better balance of rental and owner-occupied homes in the city. According to Councilmember Fazlul Kabir, 'It’s not against the renters. It’s just the added benefit of stabilizing the neighborhood,' and established homeowners are more likely to invest in their community. The council is examining adjustments to the existing New Neighbor Homeowner Grant Program, offering potential home buyers $5,000 to offset closing costs, with the goal of encouraging home buyers to establish themselves in properties that were formerly rentals.

34737.ai_summary: The College Park City Council approved a contract to purchase a property in the Old Town neighborhood for $1.7 million, with the option to complete the purchase before March 17 or face a price increase to $1.75 million. Councilmembers discussed the potential for the property to stabilize affordable rental housing and support community-focused development, with Councilmember Stuart Adams stating 'The potential purchase of 4704 Calvert Road makes sense... It aligns with our strategic goals.' Tenant D.W. Rowlands voiced concerns about the impact on renters, saying 'I hope that the mayor and council will keep in mind that renters are an important part of the community, that we are not all inherently transients with no stake in College Park, nor are we all the causes of noise complaints.'

key_people: Bibi Mohamed, Director of Helpers to Good
Azad Mohamed, Volunteer with Helpers to Good
34755.ai_summary: Helpers to Good, a program under Ansaar-ul-Birr Community Services Inc., hosted a Kids Fun Festival on Feb. 19 and Feb. 20, giving free lunches and school supplies to kids. The event was supported by the city and a national relief organization, with Bibi Mohamed noting that 'The lunch was sponsored by a grant from the city of College Park, and the gift was from one of our [partners], Islamic Relief [U.S.A]'. The organization gave out almost 400 meals and 200 gifts over the course of two days, highlighting the importance of community connections and support.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Sarah Christopherson, President of the school’s PTSO
34718.ai_summary: Hyattsville Middle School students are experiencing late bus arrivals, with up to 75% of buses arriving late in February, affecting their daily routines and instruction time. College Park City Councilmember John Rigg, who has a child attending the school, noted that 'my own child’s bus late arrival means that he misses math pretty much daily.' School Board Member Pamela Boozer-Strother attributed the delays to a bus driver shortage, emphasizing that busing HMS students should be a priority due to the disruptions they are already facing during the construction of the new school.

key_people: Denise Mitchell, College Park City Councilmember
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Kate Kennedy, College Park City Councilmember
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
34743.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmembers have been navigating meetings in different locations and formats over the past two years, including virtual meetings due to the pandemic. Councilmembers, such as Denise Mitchell and Maria Mackie, have had mixed experiences with virtual meetings, with some noting the benefits of work-life balance and others missing in-person interactions, as Mackie said, 'It's hard to read people over Zoom. I've really missed my colleagues, interacting with them, chatting with them before and after meeting.' The council has been adapting to these changes, with some members appreciating the flexibility and others noting the importance of in-person meetings, as Kennedy said, 'I understand the value of being in person, and it's important, but I wish that we wouldn't do everything in person ... That work-life balance does make it easier.'

key_people: Michael Williams, College Park Economic Development Manager
organizations: Prince George’s County Economic Development Corporation
34775.ai_summary: The College Park City Council discussed a proposal to renew the city’s participation in the Greater College Park Regional Institution Strategic Enterprise (RISE) Zone. According to College Park Economic Development Manager Michael Williams, “What we think is that start up companies and new companies are generally trying to get going and not trying to buy or purchase a building,” and the state has expanded the RISE zone program to provide rental assistance to businesses that don’t own their own buildings. The city will hold a public hearing to review participation in the RISE zone on March 8.

key_people: Darryll Pines, University of Maryland President
Julie Bringman, Pastor of Hope Lutheran Church
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember (District 3)
Patrick Wojahn, Mayor of College Park
organizations: Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission (M-NCPPC)
34586.ai_summary: The Adelphi Road Sector Plan has sparked opposition among College Park residents, who are concerned about the potential upzoning of Guilford Woods, a 15-acre wooded parcel near the University of Maryland. At a public hearing, Delegate Mary Lehman spoke against upzoning the woods, and College Park City Councilmember Stuart Adams urged the county council to delay moving the sector plan forward, saying 'We have enough time to really thoughtfully consider how to add appropriate density, how to make sure we’re looking at our school burdens, how we can preserve our green space and make this absolutely fantastic.' The plan's approval process has included stakeholder listening sessions, virtual office hours, and an online survey, with the Prince George’s County Council and the county planning board considering the next steps.

34607.ai_summary: The City of College Park aims to upgrade its stormwater management system, particularly in the Calvert Hills neighborhood, which has been severely affected by flooding in recent years. The city plans to conduct a topographical survey to examine water flow in the area and create a model to identify solutions, with an estimated cost of $600,000 and a completion time of about a year. As College Park resident Bob Baer noted, 'When Calvert road stormwater inlets max out, and the street water level rises only an inch or more, the street water goes down our driveway and into our yard and the neighbor’s yard,' highlighting the urgency of addressing this issue.

key_people: Robert Craig Baum, Wisdom 1096 Project Founder
34475.ai_summary: Maxine Gross, a resident, reflected on the pandemic and its impact on her life and the city of College Park. The city council passed Resolution 20-R016 in support of Black lives, which included an apology for the city's past history of oppression and a commitment to seek opportunities for restorative justice. As Gross navigated personal challenges, including a medical episode and the loss of a loved one, the city adopted a new resolution establishing a restorative justice commission, demonstrating its ongoing effort to address racial justice and community concerns.

key_people: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
Robert Day, Former College Park City Councilmember
34307.ai_summary: Stuart Adams was elected to represent District 3 on the College Park City Council, replacing Robert Day. Adams plans to focus on community resilience, stating 'I've always been drawn to university towns. I like the activity and being able to meet younger folks' and 'That's been my passion for a while, community resilience. I know what it's like when your family loses your home and your community gets completely uprooted,' referencing his family's experience with Hurricane Katrina. He aims to tackle stormwater management issues in District 3 using his expertise in floodplain management and sustainability.

34310.ai_summary: Susan Whitney is the new District 2 representative on the College Park City Council, replacing Monroe Dennis. Whitney aims to foster more community involvement in her district, saying 'I love solving problems, getting things done and helping people. I want to be persistent and see what we can do to make positive change for our neighbors.' She has previously volunteered extensively to support her College Park community, including giving time and energy to her daughter’s schools, parkrun, and the College Park Community Food Bank.

key_people: Mel Franklin, At-Large County Council Member
Calvin Hawkins, At-Large County Council Member and Chair
Deni Taveras, Vice Chair and County Council Member
Eric Olson, College Park Resident and Former County Council Member
Dannielle Glaros, County Council Member for District 3
34197.ai_summary: The Prince George's County Council voted to approve a new electoral map despite widespread citizen opposition, with over 150 opponents registering to speak at the council meeting, citing gerrymandering and favoritism towards certain candidates. As College Park Mayor Patrick Wojahn stated, 'The current proposed map divides College Park right through its center.' Councilmember Dannielle Glaros expressed her disappointment with the map, saying 'I deeply question the moral compass of my colleagues.' The new map affects the eligibility of potential candidates, including Eric Olson, who had announced his intention to run for the District 3 seat.

key_people: Mel Franklin, Prince George’s County Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Eric Olson, District 3 county council candidate
33953.ai_summary: A redistricting plan proposed by Prince George’s County Councilmember Mel Franklin passed 6 to 4, which redesignates portions of College Park from District 3 to District 1, while keeping the University of Maryland in District 3. Councilmember Thomas Dernoga stated, “With regard to the City of College Park, they’re blindsided,” in response to an earlier proposal by Councilmember Derrick Leon Davis. Franklin said his proposed map reflects increasing diversity in the county and remedies the separation between the University of Maryland and the university’s Discovery District, and also stated, “This process is far more transparent than it was 10 years ago.”

organizations: College Park Restorative Justice Steering Committee
34106.ai_summary: The College Park Restorative Justice Steering Committee presented their suggestions to the city council, including a framework for establishing the city’s Restorative Justice Commission, which will address harms done to the Lakeland community. As committee chair Maxine Gross said, “If you go back and look at the history of Lakeland, there are several instances where folks felt that movements by the City of College Park were simply processes that would lead to them losing their land.” Mayor Patrick Wojahn stated, “We’re doing what we can to get the word out, to get a better understanding of what happened with the urban renewal process” and that the establishment of the commission will give people the chance to talk about what happened and “make clear that there was real harm caused to families, caused to the community, caused to people as a result of what happened here.”

key_people: Danny Schaible, College Park City Councilmember
organizations: Town of University Park Common Council
34137.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is debating a ban on gas-powered leaf blowers, with Councilmember Danny Schaible sponsoring the proposal to reduce noise and gas pollution by July 2023. Schaible stated that using a gas-powered blower for an hour is equivalent to driving a Ford F-150 for 3,000 miles, and that it can be as loud as 110 decibels, saying '[A gas-powered leaf blower] can be as loud as 110 decibels, 115 decibels in the ear of an operator.' The council's decision may be influenced by similar bans in surrounding jurisdictions, including University Park and Montgomery County.

key_people: Ken Ulman, President of Margrave Strategies
Stacey Price, Co-founder and CEO of Shop Made in D.C.
Michael Babin, Co-founder of Shop Made in D.C.
34108.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has approved the use of American Rescue Plan Act funds to support the opening of Shop Made in Maryland, a retail store featuring products made by local artisans, in the new College Park City Hall building. According to Stacey Price, Co-founder and CEO of Shop Made in D.C., 'We like to label ourselves as a modern retail incubator. We provide a place to showcase talent … We provide business assistance so they can become more profitable in what they are doing, from packaging to pricing and all the other things.' The store aims to support artists and makers by giving them space to showcase their work and build a customer base, with a focus on products from Prince George’s County and College Park.

key_people: Lisa Walker, chair of Hyattsville Aging in Place's board
Miriam Bader, College Park's senior city planner
Agnes Artemel, president of Artemel & Associates Inc.
organizations: University of Baltimore's Jacob France Institute
34100.ai_summary: The City of College Park is working to become a more age-friendly community by applying to join AARP's Network of Age-Friendly States and Communities. As part of this effort, a team of consultants is developing an action plan to address issues of aging in the community, with a focus on areas such as transportation, housing, and social activities. As Miriam Bader, College Park's senior city planner, noted, 'When you can no longer drive, then it is difficult to age in place, due to lack of walkable grocery stores and walkable medical facilities.'

33771.ai_summary: The College Park Here and Now hosted a virtual forum for candidates for Mayor and Districts 1, 2, and 4. The forums allowed local residents to ask questions and hear directly from the candidates, including Patrick Wojahn for Mayor, Kate Kennedy and Fazlul Kabir for District 1, Llatetra Esters, Bob Catlin, and Susan Whitney for District 2, and Maria Mackie and Denise Mitchell for District 4. The forums are available to watch online, providing College Park residents with information to make informed decisions in the upcoming election.

key_people: Mary Cook, President of the North College Park Neighborhood Association
33836.ai_summary: The Countywide Map Amendment (CMA) process has raised concerns among College Park legislators and community activists that the new zoning classifications will substantively change the character of their neighborhoods. College Park Mayor Patrick Wojahn sent a letter to the county council expressing concerns about the lack of input from the city council and staff, and noted 10 possible discrepancies that would limit development in dense downtown areas and allow overdevelopment in residential areas. As Mary Cook, president of the North College Park Neighborhood Association, said, 'I am concerned that these new zoning classifications are going to set developers up so that they can create a new density in our area.'

33888.ai_summary: The City of College Park is undergoing several construction projects, including the completion of Tempo, a student housing project, and the University of Maryland's new School of Public Policy building, both expected to be finished in summer 2022. The city's new city hall is also under construction and is expected to be completed by the end of the year. These projects aim to transform Baltimore Avenue into a college town main street, creating a safer environment for pedestrians, cyclists, and motorists.

key_people: Karen Stewart, Owner of Jesse's Soul Line Dance
Melissa Sites, Director of College Park Arts Exchange
Rev. Carrington Carter, Head Pastor at Embry AME
33883.ai_summary: The Embry AME Church in College Park's historically Black neighborhood of Lakeland is working towards a brighter future after facing challenges. As Rev. Carrington Carter reflected, the church has 'weathered a lot of storms as far as its history' and is now moving forward with a renewed sense of dignity and purpose. The church recently issued a vision statement for Lakeland, recognizing the community's historic importance and underscoring the need for 'lifelong vitality and learning' to be supported.

key_people: Alex Keating, Director of Public Policy and Partnerships
33773.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is debating extending VeoRide's micro-mobility pilot to a three-year contract despite concerns about mismanagement. Councilmembers such as John Rigg and Robert Day expressed concerns about the company's management, with Rigg stating 'I've seen [residents] throw them in the stream, I've seen them be hit with cars, I've seen people throw them in the streets' and Day believing that the mismanagement of misplaced vehicles was unacceptable. The council is seeking accountability from VeoRide managers and exploring solutions to address the issues with improperly parked vehicles.

key_people: Stacy Hornstein, Director of Development for Republic Properties
Terry Schum, College Park Director of Planning, Community and Economic Development
Mark Vogel, Founder of Mark Vogel Companies
Patrick Wojahn, Mayor of College Park
locations: Corner of Campus and Corporal Frank Scott drives
key_issues: COVID-19 pandemic impact on hospitality industry
33651.ai_summary: Developers propose multifamily housing in place of a hotel on the corner of Campus and Corporal Frank Scott drives in College Park. According to Stacy Hornstein, Director of Development for Republic Properties, 'The financing community who would participate with us … has evaporated. You can’t get financing for a hotel today.' The proposed housing development would have smaller units marketed to young professionals and the 55-and-older community, with easy access to the College Park Metro and Purple Line stations. Councilmember Denise Mitchell expressed her support for the proposed development during the city council worksession.

key_people: Todd Reitzel, Member of the Tree and Landscape Board
33577.ai_summary: The College Park City Council delayed a vote on a proposed tree ordinance after a lengthy public hearing where around 30 residents testified, with many arguing that the ordinance would place an undue burden on homeowners to maintain the city's tree canopy. Resident Emily Friend said, 'I believe if we're going to have a successful tree planting program, it should not be punitive' and expressed concern that fines for cutting down trees without replanting new ones could lead to bitterness and discouragement from planting trees in the future. The ordinance aims to boost the city's waning tree canopy, requiring residents to acquire a free permit to cut down a tree on their property or prune one more than 20%.

key_people: Jax Chaudhry, Literacy Lab Regional Director
33567.ai_summary: The Literacy Lab, a nonprofit organization, is coming to Hollywood Elementary School to provide reading tutors for the 2021-2022 school year. Councilmember Maria Mackie spearheaded the proposal, which will account for no more than $40,000 out of College Park’s FY22 budget. As Councilmember Mackie said, 'We saw a problem. We found a solution in the Literacy Lab and we worked on implementing that solution in hopes that this small step could be the first step to helping many students throughout College Park and PG County.'

key_people: James Keleti, Commander of PGPD’s Special Operations Division
Bob Ryan, College Park Public Services Director
33505.ai_summary: The College Park City Council and Prince George’s County Police Department are exploring enforcement options to address the growing number of noise complaints from modified exhausts on vehicles. Major James Keleti confirmed that the number of noise complaints has increased, and police have issued warnings and equipment repair orders to combat the problem. Councilmember Fazlul Kabir stated, 'This issue is getting worse,' and suggested that the city and PGPD need to explore additional enforcement options, citing a joint operation in Montgomery County as a potential model.

key_people: Robert Marsili, director of College Park’s Department of Public Works
organizations: College Park Department of Public Works
33469.ai_summary: The article discusses waste disposal in College Park, with Robert Marsili, director of College Park’s Department of Public Works, explaining that every city or town in Prince George’s County handles its solid waste differently. As Robert Marsili said, “Every city or town in Prince George’s County handles its solid waste a little differently,” highlighting the need for effective waste management. The article highlights the history of waste disposal in the county, including the establishment of the Brown Station Road Sanitary Landfill (BSRSL) and the implementation of environmental regulations to protect water quality and reduce pollution.

key_people: Pablo Regis de Oliveira, EducArte co-founder and executive director
André Coelho, percussionist and pandeiro player
Patrick Wojahn, Mayor of College Park
Melissa Sites, College Park Arts Exchange executive director
organizations: Prince George’s County Arts and Humanities Council
33507.ai_summary: The College Park Arts Exchange, in collaboration with EducArte, hosted a Brazilian percussion workshop at the Old Parish House, led by percussionist André Coelho. As Pablo Regis de Oliveira, EducArte’s co-founder and executive director, noted, 'I’ve been driving by this building for so many years, and it’s nice to actually be doing an event here that’s for our community.' The events were supported by Mayor Patrick Wojahn and the College Park City Council, and marked a return to in-person events for the Old Parish House after pandemic-related restrictions were lifted.

key_issues: grant assistance for small businesses and nonprofits
30398.ai_summary: College Park is expecting $22 million from the American Rescue Plan Act by the end of July, with the first half of the funds scheduled to arrive soon. Mayor Patrick Wojahn and the city council discussed how to use the incoming funds, with suggestions including paying off the city's financial losses and providing grant assistance to hotels, restaurants, and nonprofits, as noted by Interim City Manager Bill Gardiner, who said 'Yes, we can do some additional street and road improvements with these additional funds.' Councilmembers Fazlul Kabir and Maria Mackie expressed support for funding Meals on Wheels, with Kabir stating 'This is a huge opportunity for us to invest' and 'to recover from what we have lost due to the COVID pandemic.'

key_people: Shawn Joseph, Interim Superintendent of Schools
Judith White, PGCPS Chief Academic Officer
Alexandra Tyukavina, Paint Branch PTA Secretary
organizations: Prince George’s County Board of Education
51753.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted to send a letter to the Prince George’s County Board of Education in support of continued funding for the Chinese immersion program at Paint Branch Elementary School. The letter, signed by Mayor Fazlul Kabir, asks the board to fund the full program for all Paint Branch students, stating that “The program has become an important part of the broader educational ecosystem serving College Park residents.” Some Paint Branch parents, including PTA Secretary Alexandra Tyukavina, have objected to the proposed changes to the program, citing concerns about equity and the potential impact on students and families.

key_people: Sami Saeed, public policy major at the University of Maryland
David Dorsch, College Park resident and landlord
Nick DiSpirito, UMD's student liaison to the city council
51534.ai_summary: The College Park City Council unanimously approved an ordinance that prevents landlords from asking tenants to renew a lease sooner than 180 days from the end of the current lease. The ordinance was introduced in September and drew comments from over 20 tenants, including Sami Saeed, who shared his experience of being approached by his landlord just four weeks after moving in with a rent increase threat, saying 'The stress that that caused me … trying to find anyone to live with for next year. I had to call 50 people. … That is the situation renters are faced with.' The new law will fine landlords $500 for the first offense and $2,500 for subsequent offenses.

key_people: Donald Hays, Chairman of the BPAC Subcommittee
51397.ai_summary: A citizen-led committee, the Bicycle and Pedestrian Advisory Committee (BPAC), resubmitted a year-old report on school bus stop safety to the College Park City Council, recommending upgrades to protect elementary school children. According to Donald Hays, Chairman of the BPAC subcommittee, 'I personally feel that is an outrage and there has been very little movement and very little progress' on the city's part to address safety concerns. The report included safety evaluations for 75 elementary school bus stops and recommended changes such as adding bike lanes and installing sidewalks to improve safety.

key_people: Maxine Gross, Lakeland Community Heritage Project member and fifth-generation Lakeland resident
51400.ai_summary: The Lakeland neighborhood in College Park is planning to add 10 murals to highlight its history, with a total budget of $28,382. According to Maxine Gross, a member of the Lakeland Community Heritage Project, 'It’s just a way for folks to be able to see the community that we have been talking about for a number of years.' College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir said, 'We felt very strongly that this is a way we could recognize and promote the legacy of College Park and the Lakeland community.' The project is a joint effort among city officials, community members, and University of Maryland partners.

key_people: Frances Chen, UMD Student and Presenter
Nicholas Crane, UMD Student and Presenter
Ashna Pandya, UMD Student and Presenter
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
organizations: UMD Partnership for Action Learning in Sustainability Program
51390.ai_summary: Three housing policy students suggested that the city and the University of Maryland could do more to help undergraduate tenants become better neighbors to long-term homeowners. The students presented their research to the College Park City Council, citing issues with 'studentification' of established neighborhoods and degradation of community quality of life, with Frances Chen stating 'There has … been an issue with neighborhood degradation, with many students and party houses creating a lot of trash as well as general noise complaints within neighborhoods.' Councilmember Alan Hew emphasized the university's responsibility in teaching students how to be good neighbors, saying 'The university well knows that, after two years living on campus, students are expected to live off campus... They have not taken any responsibility in preparing the students living off campus.'

51376.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering expanding permit parking to address residential overcrowding and parking issues. According to Parking Enforcement Officer Jim Miller, permit-only parking could help address overcrowding in homes because only homeowners and tenants whose names appear on a lease would qualify for permits. Former Councilmember Susan Whitney suggested the city offer more incentives, such as tax breaks, to encourage residents to own fewer cars, saying 'We want people who need their cars to be able to park them, and we want people who can get by with one car to do that and to be encouraged to do that.'

key_people: Matthew Tedesco, lawyer representing Cruz Development Corp
Carlos Nunes, senior planner with the city
51441.ai_summary: The College Park City Council agreed to recommend that Prince George’s County amend a parking ordinance to allow a developer to build a four-story, 87-unit apartment building for older residents on Branchville Road. According to Matthew Tedesco, the project 'emphasizes aging in place' and will be 'a scaled-down, senior affordable project that we believe fits not only the needs of the city but of the county.' Councilmember Kelly Jordan noted that 'there is a real need for senior housing in College Park' and the developer has 'listened to the residents … to see what would fit here, what’s needed here.'

key_people: Tom Dernoga, Prince George’s County Councilmember
51373.ai_summary: Maryland Governor Wes Moore has proposed a bill to change the state's zoning law, allowing for smaller and denser housing, which would enable developers to build at least eight houses per acre. House Speaker Joseline Peña-Melnyk supports the proposal, stating 'Maryland has a housing shortage of roughly 100,000 homes... We need more housing choices. Smaller homes. Townhouses. Accessory dwelling units.' However, State Senator Jim Rosapepe and Prince George’s County Councilmember Tom Dernoga have expressed reservations, with Dernoga opposing the proposal, saying 'As drafted, the governor’s SB 36 is a sprawl multiplier which will worsen congestion, pollution, school overcrowding and will strain public safety resources.'

key_people: Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Erica Johnson, Sgt. and Coordinator of the Prince George's County Police Department's Citizens' Police Academy
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
51366.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmember Alan Hew has graduated from three different citizen police academies, including those offered by the Prince George's County Police Department, the University of Maryland Police Department, and the Prince George's County Sheriff's Office. Hew said, 'There's a lot of speculation of how they operate and I wanted to be able to speak confidently in support of the departments that operate within our city.' The training has given him insight to share with his colleagues and constituents, and has helped build trust and understanding between the community and police, with Mayor Fazlul Kabir noting that Hew's knowledge has been important in discussions on crime prevention and public safety improvement.

51316.ai_summary: A winter storm named Fern hit the Route 1 corridor, bringing snow, sleet, and freezing rain, prompting College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir to remind residents to report icy sidewalks and unsafe conditions. Prince George’s County Executive Aisha Braveboy activated the county's snow response operation, with crews working around the clock to clear and treat roadways, and public safety remaining a top priority. The county also opened emergency warming centers and urged residents to avoid driving until roadways have been cleared, as 'The county’s snow response operation is fully active. Crews are working around the clock to clear and treat the roadways and public safety remains our top priority.'

key_people: Tina Nguyen, Owner of Queens Chapel Barbershop
Joshua Solomon, College Park City Councilmember
Kelson Nisbett, College Park City Councilmember
Merrill Hartson, Customer and Retired Editor of AP News
51173.ai_summary: The Queens Chapel Barbershop celebrated its 85th anniversary with a party, attended by customers, city council members Joshua Solomon and Kelson Nisbett, and Hyattsville Police Chief Jarod Towers. Merrill Hartson, a customer and retired editor of AP News, gave poetical remarks, saying 'For 85 years, the Queens Chapel Barbershop has persevered, never short of sheers, never at a loss for cheers' and noting that 'everyone knows your name at the Queens Chapel Barbershop.' The event highlighted the barbershop's loyal customers and its importance in the community.

key_people: Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Alex Posorske, Veo’s policy and partnerships manager for the East Coast
Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
51475.ai_summary: Veo, the company that owns e-scooters and e-bikes in College Park, has tightened restrictions on where riders can park them due to complaints from residents and city staff. As Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember, stated, “This is just almost malicious,” regarding a rider who left a Veo e-scooter in the middle of a sidewalk. According to Mayor Fazlul Kabir, “We are very happy that we have this micromobility system in College Park because this is in line with our strategic sustainability goal,” as it reduces the number of cars on the street.

key_people: Daniel Oates, College Park City Councilmember
Kelly Jordan, College Park City Councilmember
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember
Holly Simmons, College Park City Councilmember
51408.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has become younger with the election of three newcomers in November, with five of the eight members now under the age of 50. As Councilmember Daniel Oates stated, 'To me what’s interesting is less [about] the relative ages than the new people coming on the council and bringing different experiences and different perspectives from that vein.' Councilmember Kelly Jordan agreed, saying 'We’ll all have lots of questions and … when you’re used to something, comfortable with something, new questions can bring new ideas for how you’re used to looking at it.' This shift in demographics could bring a fresh perspective to the council as it works on the city's next five-year strategic plan.

key_people: Donald Trump, President of the United States
organizations: U.S. Bureau of City Labor Statistics
51404.ai_summary: A year into President Donald Trump's second term, College Park is grappling with the impact of executive orders, employee layoffs, funding cuts, and new policies. College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir stated, 'We do not know how things will actually go in the future' and 'Our policies will stay as used to be, and we will be supporting the community in any way we can.' The city has increased its financial assistance program budget to $100,000 to help residents affected by the federal government shutdown and job losses, with Mayor Kabir noting that 'Neighbors have had to take jobs that they are not trained for, and with much lower pay.'

key_people: Joseline Peña-Melnyk, Speaker of the Maryland House of Delegates
Adrienne A. Jones, Former Speaker of the Maryland House of Delegates
organizations: U.S. Attorney’s Office for the District of Columbia
House Health and Government Operations Committee
50899.ai_summary: Joseline Peña-Melnyk, a College Park resident and former College Park City Councilmember, was sworn in as the 109th Speaker of the Maryland House of Delegates. Peña-Melnyk, who replaced Speaker Adrienne A. Jones, called herself an 'inclusive' leader and intends to collaborate with both Democrats and Republicans. She said, 'This moment belongs not just to me, but to every child catching rain in a pot,' and her colleague Mary Lehman described her as 'a tireless public servant who has given 100% to the job of delegate for nearly 20 years.'

key_people: Edouard Haba, PGCMA President and Hyattsville City Council member
Monica Casañas, PGCMA Vice President and Mayor of Colmar Manor
50826.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has paused its membership in the Prince George’s County Municipal Association (PGCMA) after a former treasurer allegedly embezzled more than $30,000. Councilmember Jacob Hernandez stated, 'It’s our taxpayer money... Why would we give money to an organization that is clearly lacking integrity?' PGCMA President Edouard Haba said the organization has formed a financial oversight committee, updated its bylaws, and reviewed its dues structure, and 'We hope that we’re stronger together' to prevent future embezzlement.

key_people: Carly Zapernick, Article’s managing director of construction and development
50763.ai_summary: College Park officials pressed a Chicago-based student housing developer, Article Student Living, to include affordable units in a mixed-use building planned for Guilford Drive. The developer outlined its proposal for a 192-unit complex that will include a new church to replace the one that will be torn down during construction, with Pastor Julie Bringman noting the new space will be larger and might have room for offices or affordable housing units. Mayor Fazlul Kabir encouraged Article to use the city’s tax incentive program, saying 'The catch is that you must have affordable housing in it,' and Councilmember Denise Mitchell pressed the developer to recognize the city’s goal to bring more affordable housing to College Park, stating 'We have a vision moving forward, and we’re not going to negate that vision.'

key_people: Nisha Kumar, University of Maryland student
Scott Tebbe, University of Maryland student
Lisa Miller, President of the Prince George's Property Owners Association
Nick DiSpirito, UMD student liaison to the council
Amira Abujuma, UMD student liaison to the council
50602.ai_summary: Nearly 30 people, mostly student tenants of downtown high-rise apartment buildings, asked the College Park City Council to prevent landlords from pressuring tenants to renew leases before they have lived in their units for six months. Speakers, including University of Maryland students Nisha Kumar and Scott Tebbe, supported a proposed city ordinance that would prohibit landlords from making renewal offers earlier than 180 days into the start of a lease, citing 'predatory' lease-renewal agreements and stressful pressure to sign early. As Kumar stated, 'We've just moved in for the semester and we already have to figure out next year,' and Tebbe said, 'Either continue living in The Varsity with all these [maintenance] problems or scramble to find a different housing space and make a decision I was uninformed about.'

key_people: Samantha McLoughlin, Parent Teacher Organization (PTO) President
Carl Franceski, general manager of sales and operations for Coca-Cola Consolidated
50509.ai_summary: The local bottler for Coca-Cola gave each of Hollywood Elementary School’s 301 students new sneakers, socks, and school supplies through Coca-Cola Consolidated’s Back-to-School with Purpose initiative, in partnership with the nonprofit Samaritan’s Feet. Principal Carlos Johnson said, 'I’m just happy that we are on the receiving end,' and City Councilmember Jacob Hernandez praised Johnson for 'providing meaningful outreach' and 'building relationships in the local community.' According to PTO President Samantha McLoughlin, 'Our kids deserve just as much as the opportunities that better-funded schools get,' and the school is grateful for this opportunity to support its students and their families.

key_people: Helen Reniere, Resident of Attick Towers
Glenn Reniere, Engineer at Vitro Corp.
Charlotte Davis, Staff Member at Attick Towers
50310.ai_summary: Helen Reniere, a resident of Attick Towers, celebrated her 100th birthday and received an official proclamation from the College Park mayor and City Council, praising her kindness and perseverance. Reniere said 'I get up in the morning, I take my medicine and … I guess you got to put Jesus first' when asked about the secret to living to 100. The proclamation recognized her as a 'steadfast source of inspiration' and highlighted her positive impact on the community, particularly through her friendship with Charlotte Davis, a staff member at the apartment complex, who said 'Don’t look at [grief] as loss, look at it as love that needs somewhere to go.'

organizations: Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission (M-NCPPC)
50276.ai_summary: The College Park City Council sent a letter to the Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission (M-NCPPC) supporting the construction of a bridge connecting Old Calvert Road Park to the Northeast Branch Trail. Councilmember Ray Ranker said, 'We don’t want to spend another month waiting,' and emphasized that his constituents have waited a decade for a bridge to connect the park to the trail system. The estimated cost for the proposed bridge is about $2 million, and the council considered a less-expensive option to modify the Campus Drive bridge, but Ranker noted that this option is not preferred by residents, saying 'I have almost unanimously … have heard from those residents that [the Campus Drive bridge] option … is not where we want to spend any of the money.'

key_people: Gavin Neubauer, University of Maryland public policy student and task force member
Christina Martin Kenny, College Park Estates resident and task force member
organizations: College Park Child Care Task Force
key_issues: support for local day care businesses
50298.ai_summary: The College Park Child Care Task Force presented a report to the city council with nearly 20 recommendations to lower child care costs and increase availability in the city. As task force member Gavin Neubauer said, 'If you’re able to even increase the amount of businesses here that can survive and care for children, we’re going to be a real standout city in this entire metropolitan area.' The task force proposed grants and tax credits for child care providers, as well as a website to connect families with local options, in response to a 15.5% decline in child care providers in Maryland between 2020 and 2024.

key_people: Jatinder Khokhar, Director of Public Services
50304.ai_summary: The College Park City Council introduced an ordinance to increase fines for city code violations, including construction, fire safety, housing, noise, and unruly social gatherings, to align with a Maryland law allowing municipalities to fine up to $5,000. Director of Public Services Jatinder Khokhar and City Attorney Stephanie Anderson presented the proposed changes, which aim to ensure fairness and consistency in enforcement. Councilmember John Rigg noted that fines for noise violations should be lower than those for unruly social gatherings, saying 'Noise should be bad, but unruly social gatherings almost always include noise and it includes a lot more.'

50292.ai_summary: The Nov. 4 election in College Park marked the first time 16- and 17-year-olds were allowed to cast ballots in city elections, with six teenagers voting. Mayor Fazlul Kabir won 1,480 votes in the unopposed mayoral election, while District 1 councilmembers Jacob Hernandez and Alan Hew were also re-elected unopposed. A total of 1,598 people voted, accounting for 9.9% of registered voters in College Park.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Michael Williams, College Park Economic Development Director
50876.ai_summary: The food hall chain Wonder has opened its first Maryland location in College Park on Baltimore Avenue, featuring takeout and delivery from over 15 restaurants and brands. According to College Park City Councilmember John Rigg, 'The city has been working hard, and our economic development staff in particular have been working hard to try to really bring an exciting concept here,' and 'We want them to be successful, and we want them to be here for a long time.' Mayor Fazlul Kabir said it was exciting to see Wonder establish its first Maryland location in College Park, stating 'It’s a milestone and College Park is very lucky to have them.'

key_people: Holly Simmons, College Park City Councilmember
Kelly Jordan, College Park City Councilmember
Chris Gill, College Park City Council candidate
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember

50191.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has three new members after the recent election, with Holly Simmons and Kelly Jordan winning seats in District 2 and Daniel Oates winning a seat in District 3. Kelly Jordan said, 'It's a good feeling. I'm happy to be able to serve the residents and excited about the next two years.' Jordan also stated she will spend her early days as a councilmember getting 'the lay of the land' and Simmons plans to 'listen and learn' as they prepare to take office, marking a new era in College Park governance.

key_people: Brencis D. Smith, former council member
Holly Simmons, College Park City Council candidate
Christopher Gill, College Park City Council candidate
Kelly Jordan, College Park City Council candidate
50185.ai_summary: The results of the local elections in College Park and Laurel are uncertain, with some candidates running unopposed and others in tight races. In College Park District 2, Holly Simmons seems likely to have secured one of two seats on the council, while Christopher Gill and Kelly Jordan are only one vote apart for the second seat. The Laurel Board of Elections webpage shows Brencis D. Smith may have an edge over incumbent Christine Johnson in the race for the city’s one At-Large council seat, with mail-in and provisional ballots left to count.

key_people: Kelly Jordan, City Council Candidate and IT Consultant
Holly Simmons, City Council Candidate and Deputy Zoning Manager
Llatetra Brown Esters, Former City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
key_issues: Restorative justice for the Lakeland community
50017.ai_summary: Three candidates for the College Park City Council seats in District 2, Chris Gill, Kelly Jordan, and Holly Simmons, discussed their priorities at a forum, including restorative justice for the Lakeland community and affordable housing. Jordan stated, 'I'm just really pleased to be able to serve the community,' and Simmons emphasized the importance of reparations for Lakeland, recalling a gentleman's statement, 'I want my 40 acres and a mule.' The candidates agreed that achieving justice for Lakelanders and addressing affordable housing are key issues for the city council to tackle.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
49848.ai_summary: College Park voters will head to the polls in November to elect eight City Councilmembers and a mayor. Fazlul Kabir, the current mayor of College Park, is running for re-election and has led efforts to reimagine College Park by strengthening the city's finances and opening new community spaces. Kabir is committed to building a stronger, more vibrant College Park by expanding affordable housing, as stated in his statement to voters, 'Looking ahead, I am committed to building a stronger, more vibrant College Park by expanding affordable housing'.

key_people: Dan Oates, District 3 City Council candidate
Ray Ranker, District 3 City Council candidate
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
49703.ai_summary: District 3 City Council candidates Dan Oates and Ray Ranker shared their plans to increase resident involvement and improve public safety without creating a city police force. 'I just love our people,' Ranker said, emphasizing the importance of community. The candidates, along with Mayor Fazlul Kabir, discussed their approaches to public safety, with Kabir suggesting an increased police presence or a small city-run department, while Oates and Ranker oppose creating an independent police force, saying 'I think jumping to the answer of ‘we need a police force’ is premature.'

key_people: Christopher Gill, IT Project Manager and City Council Candidate
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
49536.ai_summary: Three candidates, Christopher Gill, Kelly Jordan, and Holly Simmons, are running for two City Council seats in District 2. Gill, an IT project manager, wants the city to become a place for people in all phases of life, and expressed concern about owner-occupied homes being flipped into rentals, saying 'I don’t want people to be pushed out of their community.' Jordan's campaign is rooted in representation, aiming to ensure that various interests are heard and incorporated into decision-making. The election is set to take place on November 4.

key_people: Kelly Jordan, District 2 city council candidate
organizations: Prince George's County Board of Elections
49533.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has voted 5-3 to lower the voting age for city elections from 18 to 16, allowing teens to vote for mayor and city councilmembers. According to 16-year-old Lovisa Jordan, 'I think it's cool, I think that it's an option that we can get our voices and opinions heard' and 'I just feel like we get to [have] our input and make decisions that impact our lives.' The new ordinance makes College Park the ninth Maryland city to allow teens to vote, with some teenagers, like Lucas Rigg and Lovisa Jordan, enthusiastic about participating in the upcoming election.

key_people: Danny Copeland, Vice President for RST Development
Orlando Velez, Director of Operations and Housing Initiatives
David Varnado, Senior Program and Operations Manager
organizations: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD)
locations: College Park–University of Maryland Metro Station
49529.ai_summary: The city of College Park offers affordable housing options, including the Flats at College Park, a 317-unit apartment complex with income-restricted units, and the College Park Community Preservation Trust, which buys and sells single-family homes to lower-income households. According to Danny Copeland, Vice President for RST Development, the Flats at College Park was financed in part through the federal Low-Income Housing Tax Credit program. The city also has programs like Live+Work College Park, which offers zero-interest loans to full-time employees who purchase a home in the city, as explained by David Varnado, Senior Program and Operations Manager.

key_people: Maxine Gross, Chair of the Restorative Justice Commission
50287.ai_summary: The Restorative Justice Commission is urging the College Park City Council to provide reparations for the harm done to the Lakeland neighborhood during urban renewal in the 1970s and 80s. As Maxine Gross, the chair of the commission, said, 'When you talk to people who experienced Lakeland before urban renewal, they speak of a vibrant community, a place for caring people who work together to meet everyone’s needs.' The commission's recommendations include offering financial compensation, housing priority, and free college tuition to those who lived in Lakeland for at least 10 years from 1969 to 1981, or their direct descendants.

51542.ai_summary: College Park officials are considering annexing multiple properties, including the soon-to-be-vacant Beltsville Agricultural Research Center (BARC) and several other nearby properties. 'We're kind of just barely in the initial stages of talking about it,' said Councilmember Jacob Hernandez, who noted that the city would consider annexing land to protect it, for economic development or to boost population. 'Should a property fall within the city limits, the residents of College Park have much more of a say in what gets built there,' Hernandez said, emphasizing the importance of community involvement in the process.

key_people: Ryna Quiñones, Communications and Events Manager
Connor Johnson, Former College Park Resident
Jhonny Ayquipa, Trumpet Player for La Rumbera Mayor
49215.ai_summary: The City of College Park hosted its second annual Bailamos! event to celebrate Hispanic Heritage Month, with nearly 200 residents and visitors attending the festivities at the College Park Woods Clubhouse. Councilmember Jacob Hernandez said, 'I think, when you break bread, when you sit down with one another, and you have that opportunity to, no kidding, just engage, it’s a whole other world.' The event's attendance was lower than the previous year, with some attendees citing the country's political climate as a possible factor, but residents like Danielle Delgado emphasized the importance of celebrating Hispanic heritage, saying 'now is the moment where we should be proud, and whether or not the Latino community feels safe enough to come out, it’s great to see that people of other communities and cultures are here to support us.'

key_people: Teresa Way-Pezzuti, Director of Human Resources
49170.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering a proposal to double the part-time salaries of councilmembers from $10,000 to $20,000 a year. As Councilmember Jacob Hernandez said, 'Everyone on this dais works incredibly hard for the residents that we care for... The responsibility can be awesome, but taxing.' The proposed salary increase, which would cost the city $90,000 a year, aims to help cover the costs for councilmembers who work hard for the community, with Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell noting that 'We do it for the love and passion of being part of the community.'

key_people: Miriam Bader, Director of Planning and Community Development
49106.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted to require owners of vacant properties to register with the city, with a $25 registration fee and fines for non-compliance. According to College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir, 'Vacant properties can negatively impact our community...  When left unmonitored, they can become safety hazards, attract vandalism and contribute to blight.' City Councilmember Jacob Hernandez agreed, stating 'It supports improving public safety...  It protects property values.' The ordinance aims to track vacant properties and ensure compliance with maintenance standards, with the goal of improving public safety and economic development in the city.

key_people: Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember (District 3)
49102.ai_summary: A city-commissioned noise analysis by consulting firm RK&K found that traffic noise on Kenilworth Avenue is 'clearly audible' in the front yards of Edmonston Road homes and warrants further study and potential noise mitigation. As resident Maria Cordone said, 'The annoyance of the noise is significant and … it does disturb our quality of life.' College Park City Councilmember Ray Ranker noted that the city is preparing to plant 80-plus trees and shrubs between the two streets to help with sound and beautification, saying 'I wouldn’t want that in my front yard or my back yard, I would want much more quiet, to be able to have conversations with people outside and not have that level of noise.'

organizations: Prince George’s County Historic Preservation Commission
49098.ai_summary: Five first-time candidates and five incumbents have filed to run for seats on the College Park City Council in the Nov. 4 election. Mayor Fazlul Kabir will run unopposed for re-election. The candidates include a mix of newcomers and experienced individuals, such as Daniel Oates, who works for the federal government as a global technology policy adviser, and Eugene Chay, an attorney who was a member of the city’s now-defunct Animal Welfare Committee.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
49093.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmember John Rigg, a volunteer paramedic, disagreed with his colleagues on using speed humps as a solution to speeding cars on Autoville Drive, instead suggesting a sidewalk. Rigg said, 'My colleagues know that I am anti-speed control devices,' citing his experience as a paramedic and the pain speed bumps can cause patients. As a councilmember and paramedic, Rigg's expertise in emergency healthcare has influenced many of his decisions, including advising on COVID-19 pandemic response.

key_people: Michelle Johnson, Executive Director of the Housing Authority of the City of College Park
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
organizations: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD)
Housing Authority of the City of College Park
49087.ai_summary: The city of College Park is experiencing a shortage of affordable housing, with soaring rent prices forcing some residents, like Nausicaä Sinclair, to move away. As Michelle Johnson, Executive Director of the Housing Authority of the City of College Park, said, 'Affordable housing is a human right' and 'being able to go to work, come home, pay your bills, pay your rent, without stress.' College Park City Councilmember Llatetra Brown Esters noted that 'no matter how much money you make, you should be able to come in this community and find housing, housing that is suitable and housing that is quality.'

key_people: Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
49076.ai_summary: Three College Park City Councilmembers, Llatetra Brown Esters, Susan Whitney, and John Rigg, have announced they will not run for re-election due to the demanding nature of the part-time job, which infringes on family and personal time. As Rigg stated, 'My falls are so busy,' and 'given that it’s fall [and] I’ve got a kid starting college soccer next week, [leaving the council] feels comfortable.' The councilmembers' decision highlights the challenges of balancing council work with full-time jobs and personal life, with Esters noting that after five years on the council, it is 'no longer the way I want to carve out time in my life right now.'

key_people: Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Eric Olson, Prince George’s County Councilmember
Tom Dernoga, Prince George’s County Councilmember
