key_issues: Shutdown of the Beltsville Agricultural Research Center (BARC)
49021.ai_summary: County Executive Aisha Braveboy met with local officials in College Park as part of her 'Elevate Prince George’s' tour, discussing issues such as the shutdown of the Beltsville Agricultural Research Center (BARC) and land use authority. College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir said, 'There are 2,600 people who [work at BARC], ... their livelihoods are at stake, but also, more importantly, many of them, when they work there, they also attend the businesses in College Park.' Braveboy stated, 'I’m learning a lot about our county... My goal is to increase our commercial tax base. That’s very important, but we want to do that responsibly.'

key_people: Sandy Lora, Branchville Volunteer Fire Co. Ladies Auxiliary
Daniel Cruz Jr., Senior Vice President of Cruz Development
Arelis Pérez, Chair of the College Park Housing Authority board
48941.ai_summary: A Boston-based development company, Cruz Development Corp., has proposed an 87-unit affordable senior housing project at Branchville Road and University Boulevard, with rents ranging from $1,300 to $1,900 per month. Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell expressed concerns that the prices are higher than in other affordable developments in the city, saying 'For us, it's kind of like, 'That doesn't sound right,''. The project has also raised concerns about noise and parking, with Councilmember Llatetra Brown Esters asking what the developers plan to do about noise from University Boulevard, and Attorney Matthew Tedesco stating that the developer hired a consultant to study ways to mitigate the noise.

key_people: Amira Abujuma, Deputy Student Liaison to the College Park City Council
48774.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering adopting an ordinance to ban software that allows apartment developers to collude on rent prices, with Deputy Student Liaison Amira Abujuma stating that 'Housing in College Park is not a luxury. It’s a necessity' and emphasizing the need for 'access to affordable housing'. The council agreed to send a letter to state legislators supporting possible legislative action against RealPage and other software companies, with Councilmember John Rigg bringing the issue to the council after residents and a former UMD Student Government Association officer brought it to his attention.

key_people: Charkera Ervin, Legal and Policy Director for the African American Redress Network
Keith Webster, Commission Member and Descendant of one of Lakeland's original property owners
organizations: Braxton Institute for Sustainability, Resilience and Joy
48751.ai_summary: Restorative justice advocates presented a report to the College Park City Council and the Lakeland neighborhood, urging the city to help the community improvement in education, transportation, and housing. The report, compiled by the African American Redress Network and the Braxton Institute for Sustainability, Resilience and Joy, details the history and impact of urban renewal on Lakeland, causing generational wealth loss and displacement of families. According to Keith Webster, a commission member and descendant of one of Lakeland's original property owners, 'Lakeland has a different look and a different feel,' and the report aims to 'resurrect the spirit of Lakeland, the Lakeland that we once knew.'

48748.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering stricter parking enforcement, including reducing the number of free permits per household and charging residents to park on their streets. Councilmembers discussed the issue, with Susan Whitney saying permits and fees will work only if enforced, and Maria Mackie noting that 'nobody likes permit parking' but it's a quality-of-life and safety issue. The council will continue to discuss the issue, with Mayor Fazlul Kabir asking city staff to prepare talking points for another worksession.

key_people: Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
48745.ai_summary: Three College Park City Councilmembers, Llatetra Brown Esters, Susan Whitney, and John Rigg, have announced they will not seek re-election in November due to increasing family and work obligations. As Rigg stated, 'I want to place a much higher priority on … family events, see my friends and neighbors more often and spend time on personal pursuits. I have determined that running for council every other autumn is a trade-off that I am no longer willing to accept.' According to College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir, their departure will be a loss for the council, but also provides an opportunity for new residents to run for office, saying 'If the same people keep running, those who are interested, they don’t get a chance. It’s very hard to run against incumbents.'

key_people: Ryna Quiñones, Communications and Events Manager
August Kenner, University of Maryland Police Captain
Rex Powell, College Park Woods Neighborhood Watch Coordinator
Alexis Howlett, Catholic University Graduate Student
48698.ai_summary: The city of College Park held a National Night Out event on August 5, which brought together residents, law enforcement, and city officials to foster a safer community. 'The whole goal of National Night Out is to get the community connected with the law enforcement who try to keep us safe,' said College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir. Residents, including Ginger Whitaker, appreciated the chance to build stronger bonds with their community, saying 'It just makes you feel safer to know who’s actually working down there at the fire station, or who’s sitting in the police car around the corner.'

key_people: Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
48651.ai_summary: Both College Park City Councilmembers representing District 2, Llatetra Brown Esters and Susan Whitney, announced they will not run for re-election in November due to increasing demands on their time from their full-time jobs and families. As Esters stated, 'My need to be present for family has become a priority' and Whitney wrote, 'My work and family responsibilities have increased substantially since the last election, and I no longer have the time and energy to provide the level of service District 2 residents deserve.' Their announcements come before another councilmember, John Rigg, also decided not to run for re-election, citing a desire to spend more time with family.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Ray Ranker, College Park City Councilmember
Stuart Adams, former College Park City Councilmember
Gannon Sprinkle, University of Maryland student
48611.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmember John Rigg announced that he will not seek re-election in November, citing a desire to prioritizes family events and personal pursuits. Rigg noted that 'running for council every other autumn is a trade-off that I am no longer willing to accept' and endorsed his colleague Ray Ranker for re-election. Rigg urged the newly elected council to push for a change to four-year terms for elected officials, which he believes would 'continue a common-sense approach to alleviating the substantial personal burden imposed by our current system of elections every other year'.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Armando Camacho, Vice Chair of the Prince George’s County Board of License Commissioners
Joshua Patel, new owner of College Park Liquors
Rajkumar Patel, new owner of College Park Liquors

organizations: Prince George’s County Board of License Commissioners

Calvert Hills Playground and Athletic Field
key_issues: Location of liquor stores near family-oriented areas
48606.ai_summary: The Prince George’s County Board of License Commissioners voted 4-0 to deny a liquor store’s application to reopen next to ZIPS Cleaners on Baltimore Avenue, citing the store’s proposed location near the University of Maryland Child Development Center. College Park City Councilmember John Rigg said, 'This is unequivocally the wrong location, and the city council stands in unified opposition to it.' Mayor Fazlul Kabir testified that 'the community has voiced very strong opposition and concerns about the introduction of a full-service liquor store so close to the Calvert Hills playground and the University of Maryland Child Development Center.'

48293.ai_summary: Former City Councilmember Bob Catlin remains civically involved in College Park, attending council meetings to share historical tidbits and serving on various committees. As Catlin said, 'I've always been interested in history and policies and why things work the way they do... I think you need to understand that in order to make your decisions in the future.' Catlin's continued involvement has been praised by Councilmember Llatetra Brown Esters, who noted his appreciation for living in College Park and his desire to stay engaged.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Julie Johnson, UMD assistant director for intramural sports and the Reckord Armory
Ryna Quiñones, City Communications and Events Manager
Cat Peretti, University Park resident and volleyball team captain
48292.ai_summary: The City of College Park has partnered with the University of Maryland's RecWell program to offer social sports leagues to residents at a discounted rate. 'Anything that gets people out and moving their bodies is a good thing,' said College Park City Councilmember John Rigg, who plays on the program's volleyball team. The program aims to bring together residents and promote community building through team-based sports, with the city allocating $30,000 to provide 50% discounts to participants.

key_people: Donald Hay, co-chair of the College Park Bicycle and Pedestrian Advisory Committee
organizations: College Park Bicycle and Pedestrian Advisory Committee
48291.ai_summary: The College Park City Council will hold a public hearing on a proposal to make it a violation for cars to park or stand in bike lanes. According to College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir, city officials have heard complaints from bicyclists about cars and delivery trucks blocking bike lanes, making them unsafe for cyclists. Councilmember Susan Whitney said, 'College Park is about five square miles, if we can't do this here, why can't we do this here? I see no reason why we can't accomplish that in College Park,' referring to creating a safer environment for cyclists, starting with roadways like Rhode Island Avenue, while acknowledging that enforcement is 'really difficult' as stated by Donald Hay, co-chair of the College Park Bicycle and Pedestrian Advisory Committee.

48237.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted 7-1 to enter into a sales agreement to purchase a 4.5-acre parking lot, known as the lower parcel, next to Driskell Park for $6.5 million. The decision came after a long debate over the price, with some residents arguing that the city would be rewarding Werrlein Properties for misusing the property. As Ward 1 resident Sean Carlson said, 'I find it galling that we would consider bailing out the developers who have not shown that they are capable of being good stewards of that land,' adding that 'the taxpayers are going to be on the hook for fixing the problem that the developers caused in the first place.'

key_people: Chris Currie, Former Vice President of Streetcar Suburbs Publishing
48233.ai_summary: College Park Here & Now, a monthly newspaper, is celebrating its fifth anniversary, with Managing Editor Sharon O'Malley stating, 'I think that local news, local journalism, is more important now than ever.' The newspaper's success is attributed to its nonprofit business model and hyperlocal focus, with every article having a clear connection to the city. According to Former Managing Editor Mark Goodson, 'It's [the community] that deserves the credit for [the newspaper's] being what it is because it's a very unique place where a print newspaper could launch during a pandemic.'

48229.ai_summary: The U.S. Department of Homeland Security (DHS) removed a list from its website that included College Park and Hyattsville as 'sanctuary jurisdictions' that are 'obstructing the enforcement of U.S. immigration laws.' College Park officials stated that 'College Park has and will continue to be a welcoming and inclusive community' and that 'The City of College Park complies with all laws and treats all residents justly and equally.' The city had not received any formal notification from DHS about its inclusion on the list, and Mayor Fazlul Kabir met with Maryland Gov. Wes Moore to discuss the issue.

key_people: Nicole Freeh, Highway Engineer and Yarrow Resident
47367.ai_summary: Residents of the Yarrow neighborhood, including Maria Cordone, are asking the city to plant noise-absorbing trees between Edmonston Road and Kenilworth Avenue to replace the ones removed for a sidewalk. Cordone said, 'I can’t hear my neighbor talking to me,' and 'I can’t even sit out in the yard because of the noise.' The residents plan to meet with the city arborist to request large, native trees be planted, and may also ask the state to conduct a sound study to determine the best solution to reduce noise from Kenilworth Avenue.

key_people: Kathryn Clay, PNC Senior Vice President
Qin Chih, Jumbo Jumbo Cafe Manager
47353.ai_summary: The City of College Park awarded $75,000 in grants to 15 local businesses through the Retail Reinforcement Mini-Grant Program, funded by PNC Bank, to help offset losses from the ongoing Purple Line construction. As PNC Senior Vice President Kathryn Clay said, 'Supporting small businesses is a priority focus for PNC, so this opportunity was a great fit.' The grant money helped businesses like Jumbo Jumbo Cafe, which lost between 50 percent and 60 percent of sales, cover rent, equipment, payroll, and lease payments, with manager Qin Chih stating 'It's helpful, It's better than nothing.'

key_people: Ethan Pham, Founder of CP Flea
Gabrielle Brinkhoff, Store Manager of Shop Made in Maryland
47336.ai_summary: The City of College Park shut down a flea market on the City Hall plaza in March after it had operated for nearly a year without permission. City Manager Kenny Young cited the lack of permission as the main reason for shutting down the market, stating 'From the city’s point of view, it’s a pretty simple issue for us: They were operating on the City Hall plaza without permission.' Ethan Pham, the founder of CP Flea, had been working with Shop Made in Maryland to host the market and was unaware of any issues until the shutdown, saying 'It just felt completely out of left field, like, I had no idea this was coming.'

key_people: Maxine Gross, chair of the Lakeland Community Heritage Project
Mary Anne Hakes, organizer of Explorations on Aging
47289.ai_summary: University of Maryland students have been working on senior capstone projects to preserve the history of College Park's Lakeland community and help city-based organizations. As Maxine Gross, chair of the heritage project, said, 'It is helpful for us to see what [the students are] thinking about, and … it’s helpful for them to see how the things that they learned about in a classroom actually work with real-life projects.' The projects benefit both the students and the community organizations they support, and students have also collaborated with Explorations on Aging to create a website for the city's senior population.

47278.ai_summary: The College Park City Council will hold a public hearing on May 6 to discuss the city's proposed $29.6 million fiscal year 2026 budget, which would increase the budget by 0.9% over the previous year. The proposal includes adding three full-time staff members to City Hall without raising residential property taxes. The council must adopt a budget resolution later this month, with a possible work session on May 13 to discuss possible changes to the proposed budget.

key_people: Dhruvak Mirani, University of Maryland Student Government Association President and Student Liaison to the College Park City Council
organizations: University System of Maryland’s Board of Regents
47268.ai_summary: Dhruvak Mirani, the 2023-24 University of Maryland student liaison to the College Park City Council, has been elected as the president of the school’s Student Government Association. Mirani, a government and politics major, helped create a pilot project for the city to give subsidies to student renters. He will take on his new role in the fall, after winning a landslide victory in the April election with 6,000 undergraduate voters participating.

key_people: Debbie Herbert, President of the College Park Moose Family Center Lodge #453
Robert Lucian, Junior Past President of the College Park Moose Family Center Lodge #453
organizations: College Park Moose Family Center Lodge #453
Iglesia de Dios Pentecostés, Misión el Buen Samaritano
Mekane Hiwot Medhane Alem Tigray Orthodox Tewahedo Church
47339.ai_summary: The College Park Moose Family Center Lodge #453 is preparing to relocate after 75 years due to financial difficulties, and is struggling to find a new location in the city. As Debbie Herbert, the president of the lodge, said, 'I hate it because we wanted to get back our building,' and Robert Lucian, the junior past president, feels 'terrible' about the impending move, stating 'We are saddened that we have to find another place, and it’s a shame that we can’t find anything in College Park.' The lodge is working with the city to find a new location, with City Councilmember Denise Mitchell assisting in the effort.

key_people: Ryna Quiñones, Communications and Events Manager
47186.ai_summary: The 27th annual Maryland Day was held at the University of Maryland, where city officials and staff participated by handing out information about College Park. 'It's building community, bringing people together in one place and showcasing how strong we are as a community,' said College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir. The event aimed to share information with current and prospective students, as well as their families, to showcase the community and make students feel welcome.

key_people: Daniel Gruner, professor of entomology at the University of Maryland
Karin Burghardt, associate professor of entomology and an extension specialist
Laura Rost, national coordinator of Bee City USA
46919.ai_summary: The city of College Park has waived the usual 12-inch limit for lawn height in April to support pollinators like bees and butterflies. As Daniel Gruner, a professor of entomology, said, 'Pollinators need floral resources in order to get the energy they need to build their broods up, especially early in the season.' Karin Burghardt, an associate professor of entomology, encouraged residents to adopt a 'not just a month-long approach, but something you kind of adopt as a technique for all of the months of the summer' to support pollinators, while Laura Rost, national coordinator of Bee City USA, noted that 'the best thing we can do to help bees is to plant native plants … and native shrubs and native trees that bloom'.

organizations: Meals on Wheels of College Park
46928.ai_summary: County Councilmembers Thomas Dernoga and Eric Olson warned that the upcoming budget cycle will be 'extremely difficult' due to uncertainty surrounding federal grants and budget cuts. 'The federal government is doing so much damage,' Olson said, noting the county counts on $267 million in federal grant money. College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir noted that the city is not proposing a property tax increase in its upcoming budget and will add four full-time staff members, including a public safety manager, with a draft budget proposal totaling $29.6 million.

key_people: Ryna Quiñones, City Communications and Events Manager
Robert Thurston, Lakeland Civic Association President
Daniel Oates, Calvert Hills Citizens Association President
46925.ai_summary: The City of College Park has started a text alert system to keep residents informed about events, news, and emergencies. According to Ryna Quiñones, the city's communications and events manager, 'We're always looking for new ways to reach our residents... and one of the ways is through text message.' Mayor Fazlul Kabir noted that the city has been looking into new ways to interact with its residents, and the system will allow for geo-targeted alerts to specific areas, districts, neighborhoods, or even streets.

key_people: Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
46916.ai_summary: The Li Chun Café, owned by Hua Wang, has opened in College Park City Hall, with a grand opening ceremony attended by community members, University of Maryland students, and local politicians, including College Park City Councilmember Maria Mackie. Mackie said, 'It’s really a … wonderful addition and a really unique place,' and expressed excitement about the new café. The café features dishes from northeastern China, including chun bing wraps, and has been popular with customers who like the 'vibes' and enjoy healthy food and teas, according to brand manager Xue Ling.

46869.ai_summary: City Councilmember Jacob Hernandez is working to engage Spanish-speaking residents and business owners in College Park, including Fidel Alfaro, owner of Alfaro Barbershop on Rhode Island Avenue. Hernandez, who learned English as a second language, helps Alfaro stay informed about city issues and supported his application for an economic development grant, which resulted in $14,000 for shop renovations. As Hernandez said, his experience helping his parents navigate society as non-native English speakers has influenced his work on the city council, where he advocates for sharing city information in Spanish, noting 'For me, one of the difficult things that I, like many others … experienced is walking your parents through every single step, whether that is interpreting at a parent-teacher’s conference or having a conversation with your parents about important documents that they need to sign.'

key_people: Jatinder Khokhar, Director of Public Services
46866.ai_summary: College Park’s city manager has proposed a $29.6 million budget for fiscal year 2026 that would not raise property taxes. The proposal includes an 8.5% increase over last year for employee salaries and benefits and adds three full-time staff members, including a public safety manager and an economic development coordinator. As Mayor Fazlul Kabir stated, 'Increasing property taxes has never been popular... This year, thankfully, it’s not happening,' and the new public safety manager role will 'give a sense of safety to our community members' by serving as a liaison between the city and its contract police officers and the community.

46822.ai_summary: City officials in College Park are preparing for the economic fallout from the recent layoffs of federal employees, with Mayor Fazlul Kabir stating 'We are not really prepared for it' and 'It looks like all of these [actions] actually came suddenly.' Economic Development Director Michael Williams said 'We’re going to just see a slowdown in some of the retail activities' and 'Normally, if you’re on a budget, you’re probably not going to do as much dining and impulse buying.' The city is assessing the potential impact of job and funding cuts as officials consider the city’s fiscal year 2026 budget, with Councilmember John Rigg saying 'It’s going to be a really tough time over the next couple of years in College Park.'

key_people: Michael Meadow, Old Town Resident and Candidate
Gannon Sprinkle, UMD Student and Candidate
46529.ai_summary: Ray Ranker, the new District 3 councilmember, took the oath of office after winning the special election with 344 votes. Ranker thanked the residents of District 3 'for your trust and this responsibility to be a good steward of your resources.' He plans to dive into city budget planning and reach out to his opponents, Michael Meadow and Gannon Sprinkle, to 'help move College Park forward together.'

46499.ai_summary: The College Park city council has broken ground on the renovation of Duvall Field, a seven-acre park for sports, entertainment, and community events. As Councilmember Alan Hew stated, 'This will be a real asset to this community and the whole surrounding area.' The project, which includes over a dozen amenities such as an amphitheater and sports courts, is expected to finish by 2026 with a budget of $7.3 million, after being delayed due to funding issues and the COVID-19 pandemic.

key_people: Michael Harris, University of Maryland student
46411.ai_summary: The 60-year-old University Club at College Park on Berwyn House Road will undergo a major renovation starting this summer, which will include upgrades to the water and electrical systems, higher-end furnishings, and outdoor amenities. According to Matt Hurson of Cape Advisers, 'This property was of interest to us because of its distress pricing, but the fact that it is a property that needs a lot of work.' City Councilmember Susan Whitney expressed concern about the potential rent increase, stating 'There is a problem with a lack of affordable housing in general in the city, but also specifically for students.'

key_people: Ray Ranker, UMD Chaplain and District 3 Councilmember
Michael Meadow, Lifelong Old Town Resident and Council Candidate
Gannon Sprinkle, UMD Student and Council Candidate
46379.ai_summary: Ray Ranker, a University of Maryland chaplain, has won the District 3 College Park City Council seat with 338 out of 504 votes. Ranker stated, 'I'm grateful that people entrusted me with this responsibility... There are just a lot of amazing people who are, in so many different ways, making our community stronger, more connected, better. I'm excited to build on that.' He plans to focus on city budget planning and will take the oath of office on March 18. Ranker also expressed his intention to reach out to his opponents, Michael Meadow and Gannon Sprinkle, to work together for the benefit of College Park.

key_people: Gannon Sprinkle, co-founder of the College Park Tenants Union and former deputy student liaison to the city council
Dhruvak Mirani, co-founder of the tenants union and 2023-24 student liaison to the city council
46407.ai_summary: The College Park Tenants Union urged the city council to investigate accusations of price-fixing by landlords of six large apartment complexes, who allegedly used RealPage's algorithmic pricing features. As Gannon Sprinkle, a co-founder of the union, said, 'Local involvement is necessary to protect tenants from predatory pricing, and we would urge the city council to investigate the nature of RealPage.' The average rent in College Park is $1,865 per month, 20% higher than the national average, and legislation has been proposed in the Maryland House of Delegates and state Senate to prevent the use of algorithmic pricing by landlords in the state.

key_people: Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Xue Ling, brand manager and owner's daughter
Natalie Horton, UMD student and Northwest Chinese Food employee
46382.ai_summary: The Li Chun Café, owned by Hua Wang, has opened on the first floor of College Park City Hall, with a ribbon-cutting ceremony attended by College Park City Councilmember Maria Mackie and other community members. Mackie said of the café, 'It's really a ... wonderful addition and a really unique place.' The café features dishes from northeastern China, including chun bing wraps, and has been popular with customers, with brand manager Xue Ling saying, 'They like the vibes, and they like being here to enjoy healthy food and teas in between work and studies.'

key_people: Denise Mitchell, Mayor Pro Tem and College Park City Councilmember
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
Anna Owens, former Mayor of College Park
P.J. Brennan, former College Park City Councilmember
46437.ai_summary: Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell and fellow female College Park City Councilmembers Maria Mackie, Susan Whitney, and Llatetra Brown Esters reflect on their experiences as women in politics, with Mitchell stating 'This is still a man's world in regard to politics,' and 'I never would have thought I would be an elected official.' Mackie notes that 'women are really coming up to the plate' in local government, while Whitney recalls facing skepticism about her abilities due to her gender. The women's stories highlight the importance of increasing female representation in College Park's government.

46342.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has scheduled a public hearing for April 1 on a proposed amendment to the city code that will forbid residents from keeping tables used for drinking games in their front and side yards. Councilmember John Rigg said, 'One thing we’ve always struggled with, one very specific piece of furniture, and that is a beer pong table,' and 'People really don’t appreciate drinking games next door … on their street when they are trying to encourage their children to have healthier approaches to alcohol than is exhibited by such drinking games.' Councilmember Jacob Hernandez questioned how much control the city should have over what residents keep in their yards, saying 'It is a targeted policy to curb [the] behavior of a particular group of people.'

key_people: Denise Mitchell, Mayor Pro Tem and College Park City Councilmember
Mary Anne Hakes, Resident and City Project Worker
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
46348.ai_summary: The College Park Woods Clubhouse has opened with a ribbon-cutting ceremony, marking a new community space for gatherings and events. As College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir said, 'This is your second home. Please enjoy it.' The clubhouse is expected to bring the community together, with resident Mary Anne Hakes hoping it 'will unite us as a city' and Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell stating that 'having this is important, because engagement means inclusivity.'

key_people: Sherry Felix, English teacher and Title I coordinator
46388.ai_summary: The College Park City Council granted $2,750 to High Point High School's Flying from the HP Nest program, which supplies care packages for college-bound students, including blankets, bed linens, and Chromebooks. According to Sherry Felix, an English teacher and the school's Title I coordinator, 'this is just a small treat' and she hopes the program will expand with community support to help more students transition into post-high school education. The grant also pays for registration fees for 42 students to join the nonprofit First Generation College Bound, aiming to increase the number of low-income students who graduate from high school and move on to college.

key_people: Erica Otte, College Park City Council non-voting student liaison
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember (District 3)
Chris Hatcher, Attorney for Landmark Properties
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember (District 1)
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember (District 2)
46331.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has asked Landmark Properties to include affordable units in a new building that would replace the 63-year-old College Park Towers. Councilmembers, including Erica Otte, John Rigg, and Alan Hew, expressed concerns about the lack of affordable housing in the city, with Otte stating 'Luxury apartments are replacing affordable housing.' The developer has not set a price for rentals in the new complex, but a similar building in the area has one-bedroom apartments renting for approximately $2,600 a month, prompting Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell to say 'We need to be more collective in letting students find housing that is affordable and that they can afford, not what you’re trying to sell.'

46336.ai_summary: A special election will be held on March 11 to fill the vacant District 3 College Park City Council seat. The city council approved the purchase of three drop boxes at a cost of $9,481.25. Voters will also select a full council during a regular city election on November 4.

key_people: Ellen Tappin, Member of Fight Colorectal Cancer and Colon Cancer Survivor
46287.ai_summary: The City of College Park partnered with Fight Colorectal Cancer to host an event on March 9, educating residents on the dangers of colorectal cancer and the importance of getting screened. As Councilmember Alan Hew said, 'Something about health and personal issues like this aren’t very well versed in minority communities, so we’re trying to build opportunities for them to learn about it, speak about it, without social pressures and things like that.' Ellen Tappin, a colon cancer survivor, noted that 'I’m hoping that people coming into the event today will become aware that colorectal cancer is the No. 2 cause of cancer deaths in the nation.'

key_people: Gannon Sprinkle, City Council Candidate and University of Maryland Junior
Donald Trump, President of the United States
key_issues: Support for federal workers who have lost their jobs
Residential property taxes and potential new projects
46182.ai_summary: The three candidates for the vacant District 3 City Council seat, Michael Meadow, Ray Ranker, and Gannon Sprinkle, discussed their plans to support federal workers who have lost their jobs and their stance on diversity, equity, and inclusion initiatives. Sprinkle stated, 'I believe that we need a strong safety net, and there have to be city programs in place to both help people find new employment and to stop as much of this bleeding that we can.' The candidates also agreed not to raise residential property taxes, but suggested new projects such as tax credits for homeowners, 'third spaces,' and eco-friendly bike and walking paths.

46022.ai_summary: The College Park Here & Now will host a forum for the three candidates for the vacant District 3 City Council seat on Wednesday, Feb. 26. The forum will feature Mike Meadow, Ray Ranker, and Gannon Sprinkle, and will be moderated by Shawn Anderson of WTOP radio. The event will be held at City Hall and attendees can also join via Zoom.

key_people: Ben Barnes, Maryland Delegate (District 21) and House Appropriations Committee Chair
45972.ai_summary: Maryland Governor Wes Moore's proposed $67.3 billion budget for fiscal year 2026 includes funding for improvements to Route 1 from Greenbelt Road to the Beltway. According to state Sen. Jim Rosapepe, 'Route 1 is the front door to the University of Maryland; it’s our flagship university and we want people, when they come, to feel good about the community.' College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir agreed, saying 'Route 1 is our Main Street, plain and simple,' and noted that the project will prioritize community input, possibly including bike lanes, widened driving lanes, and upgraded drainage, to make the highway safer for drivers, cyclists, and pedestrians.

key_people: Gannon Sprinkle, UMD Student and City Council Candidate
Michael Meadow, Engineer and City Council Candidate
Ray Ranker, UMD's Lutheran Chaplain and City Council Candidate
45947.ai_summary: Gannon Sprinkle, a University of Maryland junior, is running for the District 3 College Park City Council seat, aiming to 'bridge the gap' between student residents and long-time residents. As the executive vice president of UMD's Student Government Association, Sprinkle says 'I do believe that students deserve a voting member of the city council' and plans to focus on issues like affordable rent, food equity, and bicycle and pedestrian safety. If elected, Sprinkle would become the first undergraduate student to serve as a councilmember, bringing his experience as a legislative intern and student leader to the role.

key_people: Michael Jacobs, Former College Park Mayor
Eric Olson, Prince George’s County Councilmember
Andrew Fellows, Former College Park Mayor
organizations: National Cyber Security Hall of Fame
45962.ai_summary: Former College Park Mayor Michael Jacobs, who served for two dozen years, has passed away at the age of 81. According to Prince George’s County Councilmember Eric Olson, Jacobs was 'pretty much at almost every meeting' despite his demanding job at the National Security Agency. Jacobs played a significant role in establishing relations with the University of Maryland and was a leader in the Quality of Life Committee, focusing on updating city codes like noise ordinances, and was known for being available to talk to residents one-on-one, as noted by College Park resident Kathy Bryant, who said 'He was never too busy to talk to me, or I’m sure, to anybody else who wanted to talk to him.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
Stefan Leggin, former Brentwood Vice Mayor
organizations: Coalition to Support Prince George’s County Fire and EMS
45951.ai_summary: The Coalition to Support Prince George’s County Fire and EMS is seeking increased funding and recruitment for firefighters, with a goal of hiring 150 new firefighters and emergency medical service staff countywide in fiscal year 2026. College Park’s mayor and city council agreed to sign a letter to the candidates running for Prince George’s County executive in support of the coalition’s efforts, with former Brentwood Vice Mayor Stefan Leggin stating 'We can use this election as leverage to elevate the need for meaningful change, to elevate the conversation.' The coalition’s letter outlined several recommendations, including reducing barriers to volunteerism and improving data transparency, in an effort to address concerns about response times and staffing levels.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
organizations: Food and Drug Administration’s Center for Food Safety and Applied Nutrition
45903.ai_summary: College Park residents are fearing for their jobs and feeling the sting of executive orders from President Donald Trump, with one resident saying she has been 'shaking like a leaf every day'. Mayor Fazlul Kabir stated that 'The morale of the community is very low' and acknowledged that 'there are so many jobs of federal employees at stake', and the city has published links to resources to support those affected. The city has also issued a proclamation called 'One College Park' that focuses on the city's commitment to diversity, equity and inclusion, declaring that 'We will reject and resolve attitudes of fear, pessimism or cynicism'.

45978.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has postponed voting on a proposed charter amendment to lower the voting age for city elections to 16. Residents have another month to weigh in on the issue, which has already been implemented in neighboring cities like Hyattsville and Takoma Park. A couple of residents spoke against the change, while representatives of Vote16 spoke in favor of it, with most councilmembers previously expressing support for the change.

45975.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is forming two citizen task forces, one for childcare and one for affordable housing. The task forces will research and report on these issues, with the Childcare Task Force scouting out federal, state, county and non-profit programs, and the Affordable Housing Task Force proposing a program to offer affordable housing to those who qualify. The members of both committees will be appointed by the mayor and council.

key_people: Maxine Gross, founding chair of the Lakeland Community Heritage Project
45563.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has approved a contract with Columbia University to develop a plan for reparations to the Lakeland community, which was destroyed by urban renewal efforts in the 1970s and 1980s. As Councilmember Susan Whitney said, 'It is important to have a well-researched, well-documented, academically sound record of what happened in Lakeland and what parties were involved in that occurring, either by actions or inactions,' and 'There's a lot of hurt and a lot of harm that came from that.' The research team will recommend how the city can make reparations to the community, with Councilmember Llatetra Brown Esters stating that the city intends to fulfill its promise to commit to restorative justice.

key_people: Janeen Miller, Program Lead for Age Friendly College Park
Chyna Diaz, Director of Home Preservation for Habitat for Humanity Metro Maryland
45424.ai_summary: The College Park Aging in Place program, part of Habitat for Humanity, provides free home repairs and improvements to senior citizens, aiming to help them stay in their homes as they age. 'It's the home repair and improvements that we can help seniors with that will allow them to stay in their homes as they age,' said Janeen Miller, program lead for Age Friendly College Park. The program has upgraded half a dozen homes in the city so far, with funding from the American Rescue Plan Act, and expects to work on at least 10 homes a year, with the goal of becoming a key component of College Park's efforts to better support its senior citizens.

key_people: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Robert Day, Former College Park City Councilmember
45574.ai_summary: Stuart Adams, a College Park City Councilmember, has resigned from his position, stating 'I just knew that my ability to dedicate the time that the council involved … was constrained.' He cited the challenges of balancing his part-time council job with his full-time engineering work and family responsibilities. The city will hold a special election on March 11 to fill Adams' seat, which represents College Park's southernmost neighborhoods.

key_people: Stuart Adams, Former College Park City Councilmember
45420.ai_summary: Two candidates, Michael Meadow and Ray Ranker, have announced their intention to run for the District 3 College Park City Council seat vacated by the resignation of Councilmember Stuart Adams. Meadow, a lifelong resident of College Park and University of Maryland graduate, aims to balance development with the well-being of locally owned businesses, stating 'I want to preserve our mom-and-pops, help'. Ranker, also a University of Maryland graduate, seeks to create a 'livable, walkable, bikeable' city and engage the community, saying 'I want to think of our government as a way to engage people more broadly'.

key_people: Alan Bradford, Volunteer and Recipient of the 2024 Jack Perry Award
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
Joseph Perry, Son of Jack Perry
Martin Klapac, College Park Resident and Longtime Friend of Alan Bradford
organizations: College Park Boys and Girls Club
45413.ai_summary: The College Park City Council presented Alan Bradford with the 2024 Jack Perry Award, recognizing his 42 years of volunteer service to the community. As Mayor Fazlul Kabir wrote, 'Alan’s countless acts of service … have fostered a spirit of community and improved the quality of life for residents, embodying the legacy of Jack Perry himself.' Bradford's volunteer work included coaching sports, managing the equipment room, and working at the snack bar, where he ensured 'a kid never went without a hot dog, a kid never went without a piece of candy, whether he had the money or not,' according to Martin Klapac.

45409.ai_summary: The Attick Towers apartment building, owned by College Park's Housing Authority, is starting its third year of renovation, which could last up to five more years and cost up to $16 million. 'It's a building that's, you know, about 50 years old, and there's a lot of deferred maintenance,' said Councilmember Susan Whitney, citing insufficient funds from the federal Department of Housing and Urban Development. The College Park City Council has allocated a total of nearly $3 million in ARPA funds toward the renovation, with the Housing Authority's director, Michelle Johnson, continuing to apply for grants to keep the project running smoothly.

45598.ai_summary: The College Park City Council will hold a public hearing on a proposal to change the minimum legal voting age in College Park from 18 to 16. Councilmembers who have supported the amendment to the city charter have said that teenage voters tend to grow up to be civically engaged adults. If the amendment passes, College Park residents who register to vote in Prince George’s County and will be at least 16 on Election Day will automatically become registered to vote in the city.

45174.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmember Stuart Adams announced his resignation, citing family commitments that have constrained his availability for council work. As a young father, Adams said, 'As a young father, my availability as a city councilmember has become more constrained,' and 'I must reflect, and based on weighing my priorities, I have decided to resign.' The city charter requires a special election to fill Adams' seat within 65 days, and Adams asked the mayor, councilmembers, and residents to continue pushing projects he has championed, including the Calvert Hills stormwater drainage project and affordable childcare.

key_people: James Keleti, Maj. of Hyattsville Division 1
David Mitchell, University of Maryland Police Chief
Rosanne Hoaas, Lt. of the University of Maryland Police Department
Stephen Boehm, Deputy Chief of the Metro Transit Police
Yasmin Brown, Maryland-National Capital Park Police Assistant Chief
45050.ai_summary: Police in College Park are stopping scooters for traffic violations, including failure to stop at stop signs, riding on sidewalks, and failure to obey lane directions. As University of Maryland Police Chief David Mitchell noted, 'We have charged people for intoxication on a scooter. We will continue to do that.' The police face challenges in enforcing these violations, with Lt. Rosanne Hoaas stating that 'The reality is, oftentimes, they don’t realize that they need to be in the road and that they need to follow the rules of the road, like in a car, truck or SUV.'

key_people: Lisa Eally, Meals on Wheels of College Park Chair
organizations: Meals on Wheels of College Park
45028.ai_summary: Meals on Wheels of College Park, which moved to Riverdale in 2021, is not returning to College Park anytime soon due to lack of suitable space. The organization's chair, Lisa Eally, said 'We're doing whatever we can to find a location back in College Park,' and councilmembers still want them to return. The city council has allocated $400,000 for Meals on Wheels to use if it moves during fiscal year 2025, and Councilmember Stuart Adams stated 'The support that the city has pushed toward these types of services are magnitudes [more than] what it used to be.'

45025.ai_summary: The College Park City Council unanimously approved $7,354,000 to renovate Duvall Field, a project that has been delayed for 19 years due to funding issues. Councilmember Alan Hew stated, 'Making greatness in our city costs, and the longer we wait, the more it’s going to cost,' and Councilmember Llatetra Brown Esters agreed, 'The longer we wait, it is going to cost that much more money, and what I would hate to see is for us to vote against this, and then it not happen.' The renovation plans include adding soccer and baseball fields, an event amphitheater, and a walking trail, among other amenities.

key_people: Michael Williams, Director of Economic Development
44853.ai_summary: A Ross Dress For Less discount store opened in North College Park on Oct 12, located in the College Park Marketplace Shopping Center. College Park Councilmember Jacob Hernandez said the store will be a major economic driver, hiring 70 to 110 new employees, and provides high quality clothing and merchandise for low cost and affordable prices. As College Park resident Mona Lynch said, having a Ross in the city was 'in order' and it brings a lot more to the little shopping plaza, with Michael Williams, Director of Economic Development, hoping to fill vacant storefronts quickly to avoid 'contamination' and noting that empty buildings can dissuade potential tenants.

key_people: Keith Hooks, local representative of the National Association of Letter Carriers
44850.ai_summary: North College Park residents are complaining about mail delivery issues, with some residents not receiving mail for several days and others receiving mail for the wrong addresses. According to Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell, 'There's maybe two or three days that a resident will not get mail at all,' and 'There's some days where the mail will come, but it will be for another address.' The North College Park Post Office has recently undergone a change in leadership and is experiencing staffing issues, with many long-time mail carriers having retired, which Keith Hooks, a local representative of the National Association of Letter Carriers, says has led to an 'onslaught' of new, inexperienced employees.

key_people: Yasmin Brown, Assistant Chief of the Maryland-National Capital Park Police
Lt. Brian Wiesemann, Commander of the Maryland State Police College Park Barracks
Maj. Jim Keleti, Commander of the Prince George’s County Police Hyattsville Division 1
Stephen Boehm, Deputy Chief of the Metro Transit Police
44814.ai_summary: Theft is the most common crime in College Park, according to law enforcement officials, with thefts from automobiles and thefts of automobiles being most prevalent. As Yasmin Brown, Assistant Chief of the Maryland-National Capital Park Police, noted, 'Most of our incidents are theft-related... A lot of that is a crime of opportunity: people leaving something in their car or leaving their car unlocked.' Overall, crime in College Park is down by 11%, but property crime like theft from autos declined by 15%, while violent crime is up by 6%, according to Maj. Jim Keleti, Commander of the Prince George’s County Police Hyattsville Division 1.

key_people: Stephanie Stullich, former College Park city councilmember
Rebecca Bailey, city's animal control officer
44810.ai_summary: Stephanie Stullich, a former College Park city councilmember, has fostered 120 stray cats in four years, finding them forever homes with the help of the city's animal welfare officer and the nonprofit Beltsville Community Cats. She is one of about half a dozen volunteers who take in homeless kittens until they can be adopted, and notes that the city offers assistance to foster caretakers. Patricia Coil, another volunteer, shared that taking care of the kittens teaches her daughter about charity and generosity, saying 'You bring them in, and you never know what you’re going to get... It’s just cute to watch them … become cats over the course of, like, two or three months.'

key_people: William Pines, Maryland State Highway Administrator
Charlie Gischlar, State Highway Administration Deputy Director
44790.ai_summary: Maryland Sen. Jim Rosapepe called for swift planning and completion of the U.S. Route 1 Improvement Project during a ribbon-cutting ceremony celebrating the end of Phase 1 in College Park. The first phase, which cost $56.9 million, added bicycle lanes, raised mediums, expanded lanes, and upgraded drainage. According to Rosapepe, 'We're hoping that we can get the planning [for the rest of the project] in this coming year's budget,' and Maryland State Highway Administrator William Pines stated, 'We are going to keep going, we're going to continue to pursue grant funding through the US DOT RAISE program to try to achieve a grant for future costs.'

key_people: Joel Biermann, Former University Park Mayor
Patrick Wojahn, Former College Park Mayor
Debi Sandlin, University Park Town Administrator
Aisha Braveboy, Prince George’s County State’s Attorney
44763.ai_summary: Former University Park Mayor Joel Biermann was arrested on October 28 and charged with 28 counts relating to child sexual abuse materials. According to court documents, most of the alleged offenses occurred in October 2024, but three date back to October 2021. Biermann's arrest comes about 20 months after former College Park Mayor Patrick Wojahn was arrested on 56 counts of child pornography, who is currently serving 30 years of a 150-year sentence.

key_people: Hua Wang, Owner of Northwest Chinese Food
Xue Ling, Brand Manager of Northwest Chinese Food
44760.ai_summary: Northwest Chinese Food was named College Park's 2024 Business of the Year by Mayor Fazlul Kabir, who stated 'Their efforts have made College Park a more vibrant and diverse place to live.' The restaurant, which serves authentic northwestern and northeastern Chinese cuisine, was recognized for its high reviews and commitment to community engagement. The restaurant will eventually close its current location to make room for new student apartments and will move to the city hall building, where it will reopen as Li Chun Cafe in February.

key_people: Josanne Francis, UMD’s lead community engagement program coordinator
Trevor Muñoz, Director of UMD’s Maryland Institute for Technology in the Humanities
Xiomara Bradden, UMD Junior Government and Politics Major
organizations: Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission (M-NCPPC)
44704.ai_summary: The 13th annual Good Neighbor Day drew hundreds of volunteers to work on various community projects in College Park, including cleaning up Lake Artemesia and preserving the history of the Lakeland neighborhood. As Josanne Francis, UMD’s lead community engagement program coordinator, stated, 'With Good Neighbor Day, we are ensuring that we are catering to the needs of the City of College Park and environment.' The event also included a 'transcribe-athon' at the College Park Aviation Museum, where volunteers transcribed oral history about the Lakeland neighborhood, with Xiomara Bradden noting that 'preserving the history of African Americans, especially in this community who broke barriers, is extremely important.'

44571.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering installing stop sign cameras around the city after a three-week pilot program showed that up to 92% of drivers did not come to a full stop at equipped locations. As Councilmember Susan Whitney said, 'I just wanted to make sure there was an opportunity for public comment and really frame this in a clear and public way.' The city will collect 50% of the revenue generated from fines, with law enforcement officials reviewing videos to determine which violations will result in citations, and the goal is safety, not profit, as Councilmember Maria Mackie emphasized, 'Our motivation is safety, The city’s not making big money on this.'

key_people: Raymond Rodriguez, University of Maryland School of Public Policy Moderator
organizations: University of Maryland School of Public Policy
44540.ai_summary: College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir emphasized the importance of resident engagement in building trust in government, stating 'The key to earning the trust of the community, is engaging residents.' Kabir shared his approach to transparency and community connection, including starting a daily blog in 2009, at a 'Living Democracy' symposium on the University of Maryland campus. Kabir and Galena Mayor John Carroll agreed that mayors should prioritize local issues over party affiliation, with Carroll noting 'You're not doing it to have the power to be powerful, to be a king, right? You are doing it to serve the people.'

key_people: Andreé Sahakian, senior development manager at LV Collective
organizations: College Park Department of Planning & Community Development
44458.ai_summary: A Texas-based development company, LV Collective, is planning to build a mixed-use housing complex at the site of the former Campus Village Shoppes, which will add a privately-owned student housing option to Route 1 in College Park. The project, expected to open in fall 2027, will include 299 units, retail space, and a community space for the Lakeland neighborhood, with Andreé Sahakian stating, 'With each development, we look for creative ways to collaborate with the community, embracing its history and honoring its character and culture.' City Councilmember Susan Whitney noted that the closure of the Campus Village Shoppes 'really hit the city hard,' but efforts are being made to keep beloved businesses in the area.

key_people: Sydney Nicole, University of Maryland student
Michael Williams, Director of Economic Development
44465.ai_summary: The College Park City Council approved a $130,000 grant for Rally House, a sports apparel store, to move into the former Target storefront on Baltimore Avenue. 'I think it'll be nice,' said Sydney Nicole, a University of Maryland student, 'A lot of people around this area, they wear athleisure, Lululemon, Nike, Adidas; all that's very popular around College Park, especially with college students.' The store is expected to hire up to 70 employees and will sublet the space from Target for two years, committing to stay in College Park for at least seven years.

key_people: Carlos Johnson, Hollywood Elementary School Principal
Lindsay Sullivan, Amplify Associate Vice President
44455.ai_summary: The College Park City Council approved $40,000 for a tutoring program for Hollywood Elementary School’s Spanish-speaking students. 'They need this type of program to ensure that they’re reading, they’re listening, they’re comprehending what they’re listening to … and then read as well as write,' said Carlos Johnson, Hollywood Elementary School’s principal. 'If I could, I would try to carve [the] entirety of our budget to try and help the schools that we have here in College Park,' said Councilmember Jacob Hernandez, who looks forward to further success with the Amplify Tutoring program.

44445.ai_summary: The executive director of College Park Here & Now, Kit Slack, thanked over 200 readers for taking the first audience survey and shared the results, which showed that readers look to the publication for city government coverage, local events, and hyperlocal housing and development news. Readers also praised the publication's profiles of local community members and organizations, as well as its gardening and nature columns. The director noted that readers want to see more comprehensive news coverage, but acknowledged the challenge of doing so with limited staffing and budget, saying 'we'll keep trying to thread the needle on a tight budget'.

organizations: University of Maryland Child Development Center
U.S. Department of Health and Human Services
44448.ai_summary: College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir sent a letter to state legislators asking for a review of day care rules that require one staff member per three children if they are younger than 24 months, citing the high cost of child care as a 'significant economic burden on families in College Park.' Councilmember Stuart Adams noted that Maryland has 'perhaps one of the most restrictive staff/child ratios in the United States for infants and toddlers,' and that the required small child-to-staff ratio 'kind of was a big … determining factor for cost.' Adams predicted a change in the state's required staff-to-student ratio could take two or three years.

44437.ai_summary: College Park has installed cameras on nine stop signs around the city to collect data on traffic and parking. The cameras were made possible by legislation sponsored by State Delegate Anne Healey, who said, 'There have been a lot of people that have talked to me about people just ignoring the stop signs, just running right through them, sometimes very fast and not slowing down at all, and near misses and accidents and people getting hurt,' and expects the cameras to change people's behavior. The College Park City Council will discuss the data at their Oct. 8 meeting and could decide to keep the cameras, take them down or add monitors to additional signs.

key_people: Mike Wells, senior director of franchise operations for The Greene Turtle
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
44147.ai_summary: The Greene Turtle, a sports bar and grill, is set to open in College Park in late September, bringing a new casual dining option to the city. 'We're happy and we're really excited to be in College Park,' said Mike Wells, senior director of franchise operations for The Greene Turtle. College Park City Councilmembers, including Susan Whitney and Stuart Adams, have expressed support for the new business, with Whitney noting that the restaurant's experience in seasonal economies like Ocean City could help it succeed in College Park, but also expressing concern about the restaurant staying within its allowed limit of alcohol sales.

key_people: Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Gail Lovelace, co-chair of the Age-Friendly College Park initiative
Jen Holz, Maryland AARP representative for the Age-Friendly Community designation
Mary Anne Hakes, co-chair of the Senior Advisory Committee
44115.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is working towards achieving an age-friendly designation from the AARP, which requires a five-year process involving community engagement, needs assessment, and action planning. As Councilmember Maria Mackie said, 'I think that we need, as a city, to make College Park a place that people want to retire to and people want to continue living here.' The city has allocated funds in its fiscal year 2025 budget for a new staff member to support the effort, and co-chair of the Age-Friendly College Park initiative Gail Lovelace expressed enthusiasm, saying 'I'm just thrilled that the city is still committed to moving forward with this designation.'

key_people: Aaron Goldbeck, University of Maryland (UMD) bicycle coordinator
44031.ai_summary: The city of College Park has explored various micromobility options, including bike-sharing programs and dockless e-scooters, to address traffic congestion and climate change. According to the Union of Concerned Scientists, ride-hailing trips result in an estimated 69% more climate pollution than the trips they displace. Aaron Goldbeck, the University of Maryland's bicycle coordinator at the time, said, 'We are very excited about the growth and success of the system,' regarding the introduction of mBike, a dockless bike-sharing system, in College Park.

43934.ai_summary: The College Park City-University Partnership plans to spend $3 million to build an off-campus bike lane along Campus Drive that will connect to campus and to the Trolley Trail. College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir hopes future improvements will extend the trail north to Beltsville, saying 'Beltsville is very dangerous, actually, to bike, so that’s something we’ll be advocating for.' The Trolley Trail was recently extended in Hyattsville, and officials aim to encourage more cyclists and pedestrians to use the trail as an alternative form of transportation.

43946.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is considering a proposal to lower the voting age to 16, with Councilmember John Rigg stating 'this is something that we’ve been discussing for the better part of 10 years.' Rigg supports registering teenagers to vote, noting 'the younger they start participating in elections, the more likely they will regularly vote as adults.' The council also declined to continue a decade-old effort to lengthen their terms from two years to four, with Rigg citing the challenges of staggering terms in an equitable way.

key_people: Brian Galbraith, Cherokee Lane Elementary Principal
Kayla Newsome, Cherokee Lane Elementary Assistant Principal
Bernitta Johnson, former coordinator of the signature programs and adviser of the business program
43931.ai_summary: The City of College Park has awarded a combined $36,800 in grants to eight local schools to fund educational projects, including an outdoor classroom and experiential learning opportunities. According to Cherokee Lane Elementary Principal Brian Galbraith, 'We are grateful for cities like College Park... They give schools these opportunities to think outside the box.' The grants will support various initiatives, such as a pitch competition for aspiring student entrepreneurs and a potential greenhouse to grow vegetables for students and their families.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Varaa Kukreti, President of the UMD’s Graduate Student Government
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
Edward Maginnis Jr., UMD’s Assistant Vice President for Real Estate
43919.ai_summary: The University of Maryland is planning to build a new graduate student housing complex, Leonardtown apartments, near downtown College Park, which is expected to be completed in August 2026. College Park City Councilmembers John Rigg and Stuart Adams expressed enthusiasm for the project, with Rigg stating “It’ll be a vast improvement over the status quo” and Adams saying it’s “absolutely the type of development that we’ve been asking for in College Park.” Varaa Kukreti, President of the UMD’s Graduate Student Government, also welcomed the project as a “step in the right direction” for graduate student housing, despite noting that the new apartments may not be as affordable as desired.

43838.ai_summary: The College Park City Council approved a four-story, eight-unit apartment building on Autoville Drive, despite concerns from neighbors about its potential disruption to the quiet neighborhood. Councilmember Alan Hew praised the property owner for being receptive to the city's suggestions, saying 'It's rare that we have an applicant that's so cooperative to the conditions put on them.' Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell voted against the project, citing its potential to 'change the character of the community, the quality of life of the residents,' and expressing concerns that the community on Autoville Drive is 'not like other areas in [the] City of College Park. It is truly unique and truly different.'

key_people: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
Julia Bauer, Research and Data Analysis Program Manager for the National League of Cities
Michael Williams, Economic Development Department Director
43835.ai_summary: The City of College Park has spent $19 million of the $22 million in federal pandemic funding it received through the American Rescue Plan Act (ARPA) of 2021, with the remaining $3 million to be used by December 31, 2026. According to Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember, 'Without question, that was something unprecedented in the City of College Park.' The city focused on using ARPA funds to help residents and restart the local economy, with small businesses receiving $1.7 million in assistance, and Mayor Fazlul Kabir stating that the city council aimed to use the funds to help residents and the local economy.

key_people: Janeen Miller, College Park City Clerk
43564.ai_summary: College Park City Clerk Janeen Miller will retire on August 1 after 18 years in the position, to be succeeded by Assistant City Clerk Yvette Allen. Miller expressed her readiness for the freedom that comes with retirement, stating 'More free time to myself to do the things that I put on the back burner and enjoy life without the responsibility of waking up to an alarm clock.' City Manager Kenny Young and Mayor Fazlul Kabir praised Miller's dedication and service, with Kabir noting that she 'reminds us, she keeps us on track' and is 'very punctual' and 'honest'.

key_people: Tiffany Green, Prince George’s County Fire Chief
organizations: Branchville Volunteer Fire Co. & Rescue Squad
Berwyn Heights Volunteer Fire Department & Rescue Squad
43553.ai_summary: The Prince George’s County Circuit Court denied a request by College Park, Greenbelt, and Berwyn Heights to stop a plan to move paid firefighters from the Greenbelt and Berwyn Heights fire stations to elsewhere in the county. College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir stated, 'The damage is already done,' and expressed concern that relying on volunteers, especially during the summer, puts College Park residents at risk. The relocation of 55 firefighters and emergency medical personnel may lengthen response times to fire and medical emergencies in College Park, according to Kabir.

key_people: Michael Bodaken, Member of the Prince George’s County Council’s Rent Stabilization Working Group
Griffin Benton, Vice President of Government Relations
organizations: College Park Office of Economic Development
43581.ai_summary: A 3% rent cap is set to be approved by the Prince George’s County Council, but it may have a low impact on College Park due to exemptions for buildings constructed after 2000, with up to 65% of apartment buildings potentially exempt. As Michael Williams, Economic Development Director, stated, “My takeaway personally, and at the first swipe of it, is that because of all the new construction that really boomed since 2012, that we’re going to have a very small or very little effect on the 3% cap.” County Councilmember Eric Olson said the council worked towards a fair compromise to protect tenants while not inhibiting investment in the county.

key_people: Tiffany Green, Prince George’s County Fire Chief
43462.ai_summary: The cities of Greenbelt, Berwyn Heights, and College Park filed a joint motion for a Temporary Restraining Order against County Executive Angela Alsobrooks and Prince George’s County Fire Chief Tiffany Green to prevent the removal of career firefighters. The municipalities argued that the county's summer staffing plan, which relies on volunteers, would result in 'drastic shortages of adequate fire, rescue, and medical personnel' and pose a 'much greater risk of catastrophic injury' and 'residents in greater danger of loss of life.' Judge William Snoddy rejected the motion, primarily on the grounds that the plaintiffs had not demonstrated irreparable harm.

key_people: Mike Galvin, Director of SavATree’s consultant group
Richard Jones, University of Maryland Campus Arborist
organizations: College Park Department of Public Works
43393.ai_summary: The city of College Park has seen a decrease in tree cover on residential properties between 2009 and 2020, despite an overall growth in tree canopy throughout the rest of the city. According to Mike Galvin, Director of SavATree’s consultant group, the city's efforts to limit tree removal and encourage planting are 'great proactive steps' toward slowing or stopping the loss of tree canopy in neighborhoods. As University of Maryland Campus Arborist Richard Jones noted, 'Having forest is so critical, not only for the environment, but for our ability to go about our day-to-day lives in a city.'

key_people: Ryna Quinones, Communications and Events Manager
43119.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is questioning the future of the annual spring parade due to its high cost and low attendance, with Councilmember John Rigg suggesting that the city 'strongly consider getting out of the parade business' as the total funding for the parade boils down to approximately $47 per attendee. The city spent $34,000 on this year's parade and invested approximately 1,000 staff hours worth another $34,000. Councilmembers offered alternatives to the parade, including hosting a music festival or a North College Park version of Friday Night LIVE!, with the goal of achieving 'engagement and bringing people together'.

key_people: Ken Ulman, President of Terrapin Development Co.
Sahil Patel, junior computer science major at UMD
Heather Kingry, Representative of Primetime restaurant
organizations: University of Maryland College Park Foundation
43121.ai_summary: Shake Shack, Chopt, and Duck Donuts are set to open in College Park this fall in the new student housing building, Union on Knox. According to Ken Ulman, President of Terrapin Development Co., 'Shake Shack, you know, frankly, they have been looking for a location in College Park for a number of years.' City Councilmember John Rigg expressed excitement about the new additions, saying 'Bad businesses don’t last long in College Park... Our residents tend to know good stuff when they see it and go back again and again.' The development will also feature underground parking to address traffic congestion.

key_people: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
43109.ai_summary: The city of College Park offers multiple programs to help homeowners save on property taxes, including the Homestead Property Tax Credit and the Homeowners' Property Tax Credit. According to College Park City Councilmember Stuart Adams, 'I would say without hesitation that College Park has some of the most generous property tax credits of any municipality in Prince George’s County and some of the strongest in the entire state.' These credits can help reduce the impact of the recent 11% increase in residential tax rate in College Park.

key_people: Gannon Sprinkle, Former Deputy Student Liaison
organizations: Harford County Parks and Recreation Department
Maryland Office of the Public Defender
43126.ai_summary: Two new University of Maryland students, Erica Otte and John Fahmy, will serve as student liaison and deputy liaison to the College Park City Council, respectively. Otte stated, 'I'm very passionate about advocacy,' and aims to 'make it easier for students to connect with the broader community.' Councilmember Maria Mackie noted that Otte's desire to 'build bridges between students and long-term residents' set her apart from other applicants, saying 'She doesn't want to do the 'us and them' thing.'

key_people: Teresa Way-Pezzuti, Director of Human Resources
43045.ai_summary: The City of College Park has approved salary increases for 74 employees after a study by Paypoint HR showed they earn less than their peers in other cities in the region. According to Teresa Way-Pezzuti, the city's director of human resources, 'To our surprise, many of our employees fell below comparison,' and the city decided to aim for the 50th percentile of the market. As Councilmember Llatetra Brown Esters said, 'I think it's vitally important for us to bring our employees to a fair compensation and wage, especially if we want to maintain them,' with the city council voting unanimously to increase salaries to reach this goal.

43114.ai_summary: The Washington Post featured the North College Park neighborhoods of Hollywood, Branchville, and Daniels Park in an article about the advocacy efforts of their residents. The community benefits from engaged neighbors who know how to work together, as noted by reporter Iris Vukmanovic, who highlighted their efforts to save the Hollywood Farmers Market through an email-writing campaign. The article traces the history of the neighborhoods and notes the community's passion for walking and biking and its sense of community in part because of its tremendous diversity.

key_people: Gary Fields, Director of the College Park Department of Finance
Bonny Davis, long-time College Park resident
42752.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has approved a $27.4 million operating budget for fiscal year 2025, which includes an 11% residential property tax rate increase. As Director of the College Park Department of Finance Gary Fields stated, 'We do not take proposing a property tax increase lightly... We're looking to the future to make sure we stay financially sound.' The tax increase will raise the rate from 30.18 cents to 33.5 cents per $100 of a home's assessed value, with some residents objecting to the change, citing concerns about rising costs and the burden on homeowners, as Daniels Park resident Brian Roan warned, 'We're looking at a massive drain of families and long-time residents who wanted to stay here but decided they cannot.'

key_people: Glenn Ivey, representative in the U.S. Congress for Maryland District 4
42023.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is moving forward with a flood abatement project in the Calvert Hills neighborhood, with plans to begin mid-summer. Councilmember Stuart Adams noted that current codes are not up-to-date with climate change, saying 'We need to acknowledge that this is going to happen a lot more frequently than people want to believe.' The city is also facing challenges as businesses adapt to the summer exodus of college students from the University of Maryland.

key_people: Maxine Gross, Chair of the Restorative Justice Commission
Ruth Murphy, Lakeland Civic Association Vice President
Robert Thurston, President of the Lakeland Civic Association
41839.ai_summary: Maxine Gross, a fifth-generation Lakelander and Chair of the Restorative Justice Commission, has dedicated her time to preserving the history of Lakeland and sharing the stories of its community. As Gross said, the city's urban renewal effort in the 1960s destroyed 104 single-family homes and displaced families, and she is working to document and preserve the community's history through the Lakeland Community Heritage Project. According to Ruth Murphy, 'Maxine [is] such an incredible gift... she takes the ego out of the work, and she is genuinely committed to the work itself,' and Gross's efforts have led to the inclusion of a new Lakeland community center in a student housing development that will replace Campus Village Shoppes.

key_people: Kenny Young, College Park City Manager
Carson Peters, doctoral student at the UMD School of Public Health
Megan Ravert, liaison between attendees and the Office of Sustainability
Mark Williams, director of the jazz studies program
41833.ai_summary: The City of College Park participated in Maryland Day, an annual event at the University of Maryland, to offer information about local resources to attendees. According to College Park City Manager Kenny Young, 'I think it’s an important event just to showcase what the University of Maryland offers to the community,' and 'It gives people a chance to come in and really have an experience of the University of Maryland and its sense of community.' The city set up a tent in the heart of the mall, where attendees could meet the mayor and city councilmembers, and engage with city staff who distributed brochures and oversaw lawn games.

key_people: Rachel Romeo, UMD hearing and speech sciences professor
Susan Slingluff Hartmann, executive director of the College Park City-University Partnership
organizations: Maryland Department of Housing and Community Development
41822.ai_summary: The College Park City-University Partnership has ended its eight-year homeownership program, which awarded $15,000 to 83 new homebuyers, and has launched a new program called Live + Work College Park to support full-time employees working in the city. The new program offers a forgivable loan of $20,000 to help with down payments and closing costs, with the goal of supporting homeownership and neighborhood stabilization. As Rachel Romeo, a UMD professor who benefited from the previous program, said, 'it did make it possible for us to buy a house we might not otherwise have been able to,' and Susan Slingluff Hartmann, the executive director of the partnership, noted that the programs 'are able to offer a variety of housing solutions that I think benefit the College Park community and benefit our goals of supporting homeownership and neighborhood stabilization in our communities.'

key_people: Eric Olson, Prince George’s County Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Stuart Adams, structural engineer and College Park City Councilmember
Daniel Oates, president of the Calvert Hills Citizens Association
41817.ai_summary: The Calvert Hills stormwater drainage project in College Park is moving forward after over a dozen years, with contractor selection underway and work expected to begin by mid-summer. As Prince George’s County Councilmember Eric Olson said, 'The sooner we get construction started, the sooner it will finish.'
