The project aims to alleviate flooding in the neighborhood, which has been overwhelmed by heavy storms, with College Park City Councilmember John Rigg recalling a 2020 storm where 'the car’s floating down the street' and Stuart Adams noting that 'we need to acknowledge that this is going to happen a lot more frequently than people want to believe' as the climate continues to heat up.

key_people: Ryna Quiñones, Communications and Events Manager
Betty Colonomos, College Park Senior Advisory Committee member
41846.ai_summary: Despite a cold, rainy morning, hundreds of College Park residents attended the city's third annual Star Wars-themed parade on May 4, with participants including neighborhood organizations, the Washington Commanders' marching band, and the Greater Kensington String Band. Mayor Fazlul Kabir said, 'It's wonderful... I've met quite a few neighbors, and I'm very pleased that they came with their families.' The parade is part of the city's efforts to build community, with Ryna Quiñones noting, 'There's something for everybody, for every age, and [our offerings are] very diverse.'

key_people: Nora Urbieta Eidelman, Ethics Commission Member
Merrill Hartson, Retired Reporter and Editor
organizations: University of Baltimore School of Law
Ethics Commission for the City of College Park, Maryland
Maryland Cannabis Public Health Advisory Council
Community Legal Services of Prince George’s County, Inc.
Human Rights Commission for Prince Georges’ County
42280.ai_summary: The article lists the board of directors for a local organization, including Marta McLellan Ross as President, Melanie Dzwonchyk as Secretary, and Joe Murchison as Treasurer. The board members have diverse backgrounds and experience in fields such as journalism, law, and community service. Nora Urbieta Eidelman, a member of the Ethics Commission for the City of College Park, Maryland, was previously awarded the William L. Marbury Outstanding Advocate Award for her work in representing the civil legal needs of low-income Marylanders.

key_people: Kelly Jordan, co-president of the Berwyn District Civic Association
Bob Catlin, co-president of the Berwyn District Civic Association
Jacob Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
41659.ai_summary: The Berwyn community in College Park has been publishing a regular newsletter for 50 years, and two years ago, the Berwyn District Civic Association started translating it into Spanish. 'It always had been in English, and then we had enough of a Spanish-speaking population here' that offering the newsletter in two languages made sense, according to Kelly Jordan, co-president of the association. The city has proposed a budget item to provide $7,500 for neighborhood associations to pay for translators, and College Park City Councilmember Jacob Hernandez said, 'I’m all about getting folks who speak Spanish to be a part of the larger community.'

41342.ai_summary: The Hollywood Farmers Market in North College Park will open for the season on April 13, despite concerns from city staff about its cost, with the city considering increasing the market's budget to $30,000 next year. Assistant City Manager Bill Gardiner noted that 'In the big scheme of our budget, it's not very much money,' but added that 'At the same time, in terms of comparable farmers markets, it's still unusual.' Mayor Fazlul Kabir said that many residents are 'very passionate' about the market and 'want to come and see other community members,' highlighting its value for community engagement.

41366.ai_summary: Mayor Fazlul Kabir highlighted College Park's 'exponential growth' in 2023, citing the opening of 10 businesses that will bring 250 new jobs to the area. The city is expecting nearly 20 new businesses and restaurants to open in the coming year, and will add two student housing complexes and three affordable housing complexes through grants and partnerships. Kabir also noted the city's efforts to improve sustainability, diversity, and safety, including sizable donations to community organizations.

41323.ai_summary: The College Park city council is considering a 13% increase in residential property taxes, which would be the first increase in 10 years, to help fund a $27.4 million budget proposal for fiscal year 2025. According to Gary Fields, the city's finance director, 'It's a reset, we're doing it for the future. … We feel like that we will not have to be looking at [another] rate increase in the near future.' The proposed increase would add $156 a year to the property tax bill of a $390,000 home, the approximate average value of a residence in College Park.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
Dhruvak Mirani, Student Liaison to the College Park City Council
Gannon Sprinkle, Deputy Student Liaison to the College Park City Council
key_issues: Student representation on the city council
41358.ai_summary: The City of College Park is accepting applications for a student liaison to the city council until April 12. According to Mayor Fazlul Kabir, 'The ideal candidate would attend the meetings, work with [city council] in a collaborative fashion and who has a genuine concern about College Park on both sides of Route 1.' Current student liaison Dhruvak Mirani noted that 'Our city ought to be responsive to student interests, but sometimes it’s really hard to believe that, see[ing] it play out in reality,' and expressed his desire for the city council to give voting status to the student liaisons.

41348.ai_summary: The Maryland General Assembly passed two bills to increase penalties for street racing and allow Prince George's and Montgomery counties to install noise-sensing traffic cameras. College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir stated that 'It is not unusual for groups of motorcycle and ATV drivers to briefly take over sections of Baltimore Avenue on Route 1' and that 'These actions harm our communities, and these actions waste … resources, especially the police resources.' Del. Mary Lehman said that 'The fact is, exhibition driving will continue to disrupt our communities, endanger law enforcement and risk serious bodily harm, or worse, to participants and passersby.'

41374.ai_summary: The City of College Park is investigating its racial equity officer, Kayla Carter, after news reports revealed she posted offensive remarks on her personal social media accounts, including calling police 'white supremacists'. City officials declined to comment on the investigation except to say that Carter's views 'do not reflect the opinions, beliefs and core values of the City of College Park and its mayor and council', as stated in a press release from City Manager Kenny Young. The investigation highlights the city's efforts to address systemic racism, as Carter was hired in 2022 to examine city policies and programs for barriers to equal participation and identify opportunities for Black residents.

key_people: Gregory Slater, Former Maryland Transportation Secretary
Kayla Scott, manager at Looney’s Pub
41191.ai_summary: The first phase of the Route 1 improvement project is expected to finish this spring after a year-long delay, according to Derek Gunn, SHA’s District 3 engineer. The project, which began in 2020, includes adding bike lanes, raised medians, and turn lanes to Route 1. College Park Mayor Fazlul Kabir said, “The changes, once it’s happened, will make Route 1 very nice,” and “It’s not about making the street pretty, … It’s for our safety.”

key_people: Danny Copeland, RST Development Vice President
Kate Kennedy, District 1 resident and former city councilmember
organizations: Meals on Wheels of College Park
41187.ai_summary: Developers of a 317-unit affordable apartment building on Baltimore Avenue, Flats at College Park, expect to start construction as soon as April. The project will offer two- and three-bedroom apartments with monthly rents based on income, targeting households that make between 50% to 80% of the area median income. According to Danny Copeland, RST Development Vice President, 'It’s a redevelopment of some motels that weren’t necessarily a shining light along the Route 1 corridor,' and the new property will revitalize the community while putting the space to better use with affordable housing for low-income tenants.

key_people: Griffin Limerick, Life & Times managing editor
41131.ai_summary: The city of College Park voted to explore imposing a tax on vacant and/or blighted properties, as it can be more profitable for owners and developers to keep a property vacant. The city is hoping to identify the right tax structure and language to make that math work more towards ending vacancies. Griffin Limerick, Life & Times managing editor, is calling for a return of in-person council meetings in Hyattsville, noting that neighboring cities such as College Park have already returned to some combination of in-person and virtual meetings.

key_people: Kenny Young, College Park City Manager
key_issues: Tax on vacant and blighted properties
40635.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted to form a subcommittee to explore taxing blighted and vacant properties in the city. According to College Park City Manager Kenny Young, 'We’re in a position now that [we] kind of have to force the issue and get people to take more of an interest in making sure properties are maintained in College Park.' Councilmember Stuart Adams noted that 'The intent of the vacant and blighted property tax discussion, substantially in the commercial space but also in the residential space, is to mitigate some of the tax incentives that can occur for property owners to keep things vacant.'

key_people: Maxine Gross, Chairwoman of the Restorative Justice Commission
Susan Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
Joanne Braxton, CEO and President of the Braxton Institute
40745.ai_summary: The College Park Restorative Justice Commission has partnered with the Braxton Institute to host a series of lectures and workshops in Lakeland, focusing on the community's history and restorative justice. As Maxine Gross, chairwoman of the commission, stated, 'if you don’t learn your history, you’re destined to repeat it' and 'it’s important to not just recognize that thing happened, but also to take positive action on assisting that community and heal it.' The events aim to raise awareness about urban renewal in Lakeland and its impact on the community, with College Park City Councilmember Susan Whitney saying the program will help residents understand the impact of urban renewal on Lakeland.

key_people: Bonnie McClellan, Vice Chair of the College Park Woods Neighborhood Watch
Robert Marsili, College Park Director of Public Works
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
40680.ai_summary: The College Park City Council approved a $1.3 million contract with Rainbow Construction Co. to build a new community center in College Park Woods, with construction expected to begin in March and finish by the end of the year. This project has been in the works since 2018, when the city bought the old College Park Woods Swim Club facility, and will provide indoor space for gatherings and events. As Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember, said, 'Many of our activities have been underneath the gazebo in the pouring rain, so it’ll be a nice thing to have for the District 4 community, but also for the city.'

organizations: Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission
key_issues: Renaming of parks and community centers
Establishment of the Lakeland Legacy Scholarship Fund
40750.ai_summary: The College Park Restorative Justice Commission proposed changes to park names to honor Lakeland history, including renaming Lake Artemesia Natural Area and the College Park Community Center. The commission also called for the establishment of a scholarship fund for Lakeland residents, with Kaya Carter stating that these changes would 'not only preserve [the Lakeland] legacy but also educate future generations about the important role that this area has played in shaping the city.' Councilmember Jacob Hernandez expressed appreciation for the commission's efforts, saying 'I just want to thank you all for the very thoughtful steps to try to address some of the harder parts of our history here in College Park, but also trying to pay homage to the brighter future that we have.'

40533.ai_summary: The article mentions the City of Laurel's event celebrating women for Women's History Month, while College Park has a static exhibit at city hall. The Washington Commanders' headquarters is moving to the University of Maryland's Discovery District on River Road, which may attract other prominent companies. A recent PGCPS board meeting focused on bussing and transportation, with one parent stating their child's bus had been at least 30 minutes late to school daily, and Board Member Pamela Boozer-Strother noted that the district has been unwilling to change bell times despite previous recommendations.

key_people: Dhruvak Mirani, University of Maryland Student and Liaison to the College Park City Council
Gannon Sprinkle, University of Maryland Student and Deputy Liaison to the College Park City Council
William Edwards, University of Maryland Student
40245.ai_summary: Two University of Maryland student leaders, Dhruvak Mirani and Gannon Sprinkle, proposed establishing a tenants union to lower the cost of rent, improve living conditions, and address maintenance issues in College Park. As Mirani said, 'We can’t really address housing prices in a vacuum without talking about all of the other issues that go along with it,' and Sprinkle added, 'We need something to rally behind, a force to fight for better living conditions and lower rent prices.' The students plan to start the union, with Mirani describing it as a 'soft launch,' and have begun meeting with similar groups, such as Stomp Out Slumlords, to discuss how to proceed.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
Denise Mitchell, Mayor Pro Tem and District 4 Councilmember
39395.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has proposed a housing subsidy of $1,500 for each of 150 low-income University of Maryland undergraduates to help them rent apartments in high-rise Route 1 buildings. Mayor Fazlul Kabir stated, 'I'm not quite sure what is going to happen with this proposal at the end, given all the pushback and questions,' after an informational public meeting where many residents questioned the plan's dedication of $225,000 to the program. The proposal aims to cover one month's rent for each recipient, with a 3% tax increase on commercial, industrial, and apartment buildings paying for the subsidy if approved.

organizations: College Park Committee for a Better Environment (CBE)
39385.ai_summary: The College Park Committee for a Better Environment (CBE) has been leading sustainability efforts in the city for over 50 years. The committee has partnered with the College Park Arts Exchange on a project to paint murals on storm drains, with local artists having painted murals on 14 storm drains, and plans to get either a marker or art on every storm drain in the city. Todd Larson, CBE chair, said, “This project gives local artists a chance to show their work in a place where everyone can see it, while also providing an educational message to help residents understand why it’s important not to put anything down the storm drain except water.”

key_people: Emily Friend, Berwyn Resident and BDCA Treasurer
39270.ai_summary: The City of College Park is exploring possible uses for a vacant lot in Berwyn, including designating it as a pocket park, dog park, or community garden. Rakhmel Rafi, the city's grants coordinator, presented options to residents, who expressed enthusiasm for a permaculture garden but also raised concerns about maintenance, with Emily Friend suggesting a rain garden as an alternative. As Dan Blasberg noted, 'The city's track record of doing water management projects, to me, is an abject failure,' highlighting the need for effective storm water management in the area.

key_people: Eric Olson, Prince George’s County Councilmember
39177.ai_summary: New laws are set to take effect in Maryland starting Jan. 1, 2024, including a plastic bag ban in Prince George’s and Anne Arundel counties, similar to the one adopted by the City of College Park last year. The state minimum wage will also increase to $15 per hour, affecting over 160,000 workers, with Maryland Del. Joseline Pena-Melnyk stating that 'people are barely getting by. Even $15 an hour is not enough; we should have a living wage. But it’s a balancing act between the needs of people and what the businesses can handle right now.' Additionally, health insurers in Maryland will be required to cover biomarker testing for cancer detection and treatment, and a new law restricts telephone solicitations, prohibiting the use of automated systems to make calls to solicit people, with Prince George’s County Councilmember Eric Olson stating that 'plastic pollution is a real problem,' and the plastic bag ban will 'remove over 300 million plastic bags a year from the waste stream in Prince George’s County alone.'

key_people: Millard House II, Superintendent of Prince George's County Public Schools
NKenge Baker, Principal of Northwestern High School
Ashanti Martinez, Member of the state's House of Delegates
organizations: University of Maryland is not mentioned, but local schools and government are discussed
key_issues: Cyberattack on Prince George's County Public Schools
Environmental concerns from cargo train derailment
39145.ai_summary: The article reviews 2023, which ended in tragedy for local county schools after two Riverdale Elementary School students were struck and killed by a van. Parent leadership groups expressed concerns about unfilled crossing guard positions and pedestrian safety around schools. Additionally, the article mentions the Prince George's County Public Schools bus driver shortages, a cyberattack on the school system, and the opening of the new Hyattsville Middle School, which helped ease the strain of the bus driver shortage.

39012.ai_summary: Jacob T. Hernandez has joined the College Park City Council as a District 1 representative, aiming to enhance communication, improve education, and help residents navigate bureaucratic hurdles. Hernandez, the city's only Hispanic American councilmember, says 'It's only through our united organized voices that we are going to be able to facilitate the greater projects and programs that I have in store' and hopes to strengthen relationships between the city and local public education leaders. Hernandez will represent District 1 alongside Councilmember Alan Hew, who looks forward to working with Hernandez on further integrating the Latino community in College Park.

key_people: Matt Tedesco, Attorney for Greenhill Capital Corporation
Richard Biffl, Chair of the Historic District Local Advisory Committee
Adele Ellis, Patron of Pho Tom
organizations: Old Town Historic District Local Advisory Committee
39027.ai_summary: The proposed Terrapin House development in downtown College Park would displace three local restaurants, Pho Tom, Northwest Chinese, and Ritchie’s Colombian Restaurant, to make way for a six-story mixed-use building with apartments and commercial space. The project has raised concerns among residents and local officials about the impact on existing restaurants, historic character, and stormwater management, with Adele Ellis saying 'We’re losing too many local restaurants' and advocating for the developer to make the rent affordable for the existing restaurants to return. The development plan has been subject to multiple public meetings and has been revised to address some of the concerns, including stormwater management, with Matt Tedesco stating that the project 'will actually facilitate better stormwater management and reduce flooding'.

key_people: Michael Williams, College Park’s economic development director
39023.ai_summary: The City of College Park is responding to small business losses due to new apartment buildings and student housing developments, with a $75,000 business retention fund in the fiscal 2024 budget. As Michael Williams, College Park’s economic development director, stated, 'We wanted to retain some of the businesses who make up the fabric and the character of College Park.' The city has already allocated $23,000 to Laser Essential for relocation assistance, and Councilmember Stuart Adams noted that around half of the funds raised by the commercial property tax increase could reach local businesses, saying 'It could be more, the council will have to make a determination on that in a future meeting.'

key_people: H.C. Byrd, University of Maryland President
organizations: Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission (M-NCPPC)
39017.ai_summary: The city of College Park has a history of deliberate government policies that led to the destruction of the Lakeland community, where Black families had built homes, schools, and businesses. As the author, Maxine Gross, notes, 'the prosperity of Lakeland, Berwyn, Hollywood and every neighborhood in the city determines whether the whole city will thrive.' The University of Maryland's president, Dr. H.C. 'Curley' Byrd, initiated a plan to create a new municipality, College Park, which led to the displacement of many Lakeland residents, and the area was later targeted for urban renewal, resulting in the loss of homes and community.

39032.ai_summary: The Winter Wonderland event was held at city hall plaza on Dec. 2, featuring music, food, and activities for all ages. Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell and Councilmember Llatetra Brown Esters flipped a giant red switch to light up a 20-foot tall holiday tree. Old Town resident Kathy Bryant commented that the new city hall was a wonderful venue for the event, saying 'We couldn’t have had something like this before,' and Berwyn resident Emily Friend said her 5-month-old son Miles had a good time, noting 'It’s cool that there was something fun for every group and every age.'

39010.ai_summary: The College Park Here & Now is seeking donations to support local news, with a goal of raising $13,000 by the end of the year. As Kit Slack, Executive Director of Streetcar Suburbs Publishing, notes, 'If just 90 of our 30,000 readers in College Park chip in $100, we will meet our goal!' The publication relies on donations to deliver news to every home in the city, every month, and hopes to add Spanish content in the coming year with sufficient support.

38926.ai_summary: City Councilmember Emily Strab won the first-ever Maryland Catholic Woman of the Year award, presented by the Maryland Catholic Women’s Conference. According to the conference, “Emily Strab’s selection as the 2023 Maryland Catholic Woman of the Year exemplifies the values of faith, hope, and love in action.” This award recognizes Strab's contributions to the community, and she will continue to serve on the city council until her term expires in 2025.

key_people: Sara Rían, Author and Grief Poet
38992.ai_summary: The author, Jaclyn Bruner, urges the College Park City Council to help advocate for crossing guards for Hollywood Elementary School, citing an incident where two Riverdale Elementary School students were hit by a car. As Sara Rían writes, “grow up. get old. / what a privilege. / what a gift,” Bruner emphasizes the importance of protecting children's safety. With 83 crossing guard positions currently open in Prince George’s County, the author calls on the city council to address the issue, which has been exacerbated by the pandemic and staffing shortages.

key_people: Robert "RC" Carter, head of the ElectionGuard program
Benjamin Hovland, member of the College Park Board of Elections Supervisors
organizations: College Park Board of Elections Supervisors
38704.ai_summary: The City of College Park has implemented new verification technology in its elections, developed by Microsoft, which allows voters to confirm that their votes have been counted. As Robert "RC" Carter, head of the ElectionGuard program, explained, "Think of it as like FedEx saying your package has arrived." The technology has been tested in smaller elections in Fulton County, Wisconsin, and Preston, Idaho, and is intended to increase confidence and participation in election outcomes. The implementation of this technology in College Park is a result of collaboration among the city, ElectionGuard, and other partners.

key_issues: city ordinance on grass and weeds
38718.ai_summary: The College Park city council has passed a new ordinance on grass and weeds, permitting alternative gardens and the use of native plants. A resident, Mowed and Manicured on Madison Street, expressed aesthetic reservations about the new ordinance, stating 'it seems to me there’s no way such a garden can look anything but messy and unkempt.' Miss Floribunda, in response, highlighted the popularity of native plants and tall grasses in French gardens, suggesting that they can add a charming and loosened look to traditional formal gardens.

key_people: Jacob T. Hernandez, College Park City Councilmember
Kate Kennedy, College Park City Councilmember
Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Kamthorn G. Clay, College Park City Council candidate
Bryan Haddad, College Park City Council candidate
Brian J. Roan, College Park City Council candidate
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
Stuart M. Adams, College Park City Councilmember
John B. Rigg III, College Park City Councilmember
A. Perez Abbott, College Park City Council candidate
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
Susan L. Whitney, College Park City Councilmember
Maria E. Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Denise C. Mitchell, College Park City Councilmember
38626.ai_summary: Jacob T. Hernandez was elected to the College Park City Council for District 1 with 266 votes, while Alan Hew was reelected with 322 votes. Incumbent Mayor Fazlul Kabir ran unopposed and was reelected with 1,217 votes. The city also held a nonbinding vote on term lengths for councilmembers, with 710 voters supporting a change to staggered 4-year terms and 567 opposing the proposed change.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Perez Abbott, College Park City Council Candidate
organizations: The U.S. Biomedical Advanced Research and Development Authority
Prince George’s County Economic Development Corporation
38536.ai_summary: The College Park District 3 city council candidates and Mayor Fazlul Kabir discussed economic development, public safety, and property taxes at a forum sponsored by the Yarrow Civic Association. The candidates, including Stuart Adams, John Rigg, and Perez Abbott, addressed the displacement of 15 businesses at Campus Village Shops and the need to support local businesses. Mayor Kabir stated, 'We have been working to take care of the businesses because they are our assets,' and discussed the possibility of an independent police force, saying it would be acceptable if it did not raise residents' taxes.

38531.ai_summary: The College Park Here & Now sponsored a candidate forum for District 1 in the council chambers at City Hall on October 18, 2023. The forum covered various topics including government teamwork, small business development, construction and development, climate change and biodiversity, and road dangers. Voting for the College Park election will be on November 5 at the College Park Community Center.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
38455.ai_summary: The City of College Park is holding an election on November 5, with early voting on October 25 and November 2. Mayor Fazlul Kabir is running for re-election, with a platform focused on uniting the community, enhancing transparency, and fostering trust. Kabir's advocacy focuses on improving the quality of life with strong public safety measures, enhanced code enforcement, smart economic development, and support for new small businesses, as he states 'Looking ahead, Dr. Kabir plans to continue leading College Park by uniting the community, enhancing transparency and fostering trust.'

key_people: Daniel Cunningham, Executive Director of the Community Preservation Trust
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
Susan Hartman, Executive Director of the College Park City-University Partnership
organizations: Maryland Department of Housing and Community Development
38473.ai_summary: The Community Preservation Trust, an offshoot of the College Park City-University Partnership, has celebrated its first home purchase and sale in the city. According to Daniel Cunningham, Executive Director of the trust, 'By providing opportunities to expand the pool of homeowners, we have an opportunity for more diversity of homeownership, long-term preservation of neighborhoods and also creating wealth opportunities for people.' Mayor Fazlul Kabir praised the program, stating 'This is a fantastic event … and this is a program that will give our residents an opportunity to own a home, build generation wealth and help stabilize the neighborhood.' The purchase was made possible through grants from the Maryland Department of Housing and Community Development and the Metropolitan Washington Council of Governments.

key_people: Emily Clipton, Low Impact Design Center
Lesley Riddle, Hyattsville Director of Public Works
38376.ai_summary: The College Park city council discussed plans for the new King Park, which is currently closed and undergoing renovations. Emily Clipton from the Low Impact Design Center presented a plan that took into consideration feedback from residents, and noted that the final design returns the playground to its original location at the side of the park to allow enough green space for residents to enjoy. Lesley Riddle, Hyattsville Director of Public Works, said 'We're in good shape,' after ensuring that an underground heating oil tank had been properly remediated, and is optimistic that the park can reopen in late Spring 2024.

key_people: Miriam Bader, Director of Planning and Community Development
38373.ai_summary: The College Park City Council unanimously approved the street name Ancestor’s Lane, a decision made in collaboration with the Restorative Justice Commission, which aims to combat racial inequality in the Lakeland community. As Kayla Carter, Racial Equity Officer, explained, 'The Lakeland community, in particular, was historically Black,' and the commission chose a name that the entire community could connect with. The commission hopes to work towards reparations and build community engagement, with Carter stating 'It’s a very large project, and things are still new, but we’re doing the best we can.'

38371.ai_summary: The College Park election will take place on November 5. The article provides resources for voters, including a Voters Guide and links to candidate forums for District 1 and District 3. The article invites readers to check back for the latest updates on the election.

38290.ai_summary: The College Park city council is preparing for a November 5 election, which will include a non-binding advisory question about switching from two-year to four-year terms for elected officials. Councilmember Stuart Adams noted that 'If the people vote yes, the city won’t actually change things until the council votes on it. The ballot question’s purpose is to gather information.' Resident Aubrey Batten expressed concern that the proposal is 'advantageous to candidates and incumbents' and that 'our voice was made clear by the 2019 vote that we prioritize accountability over making it easy to campaign.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
Alexa Bely, teacher/researcher at the University of Maryland
38056.ai_summary: The current voting system in College Park has a flaw where voter preferences among candidates are ignored, which can lead to a candidate being elected without the broadest appeal. Jeremy Rose and Alexa Bely propose adopting ranked choice voting, also known as instant runoff voting, which considers a voter's top preference and their ranked choices across all candidates. They suggest that College Park should consider adopting ranked choice voting for the 2025 city election, as it can increase overall voter satisfaction and decrease polarization among candidates.

key_people: Matthew Kirschebaum, English professor at the University of Maryland
38050.ai_summary: Two new franchise restaurants, The Great Greek Mediterranean Grill and The Little Blue Menu, are coming to College Park, sparking community debate. The Little Blue Menu, a digital-based restaurant, will be located on the former site of the Applebee's on Baltimore Avenue and will offer competitive-wage employment and give back to the community, with a $25,000 donation to Feeding America. As Matthew Kirschebaum said, 'There is absolutely no dissembling whatsoever. [Chick-fil-A] simply has no interest in the community and isn’t going to even try to pretend otherwise,' while Elizabeth Maring expressed 'sadness and frustration,' and Christina Grant noted that ordering through an app might be challenging for some.

key_people: John Kish, Chairman of the Election Board
Martin Mitchell, City Council Member and Mayoral Candidate
Brencis Smith, City Council Member and Mayoral Candidate
Keith Sydnor, City Council Member and Mayoral Candidate
38039.ai_summary: A temporary ban on campaigning by candidates for city office has caused confusion among three announced mayoral candidates, including Martin Mitchell, Brencis Smith, and Keith Sydnor. As Sydnor noted, he began campaigning on May 1 and filed his packet the same day, except for the finance report, which was to be submitted later, saying 'It was never an issue' in his previous council campaigns. The candidates have been affected by the rule, with one of them, Keith Sydnor, being hit with a $1,000 fine, and another, Brencis Smith, distributing campaign literature during the city’s Independence Day parade, while Martin Mitchell has contested the no-campaign rule in a letter to John Kish, Chairman of the Election Board.

key_people: Alan Hew, College Park City Councilmember
Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
Joel Carlson, Friend of Alan Hew
Marcus Afzali, Former College Park City Councilmember
organizations: College Park Committee for a Better Environment
37990.ai_summary: Alan Hew was sworn in as the new College Park City Councilmember for District 1, bringing his experience in environmental education and community engagement to the role. Hew has a strong background in animal welfare and pollinator conservation, having co-chaired the College Park Animal Welfare Committee and chaired the Bee City USA Committee. As Hew recalled, former councilmember Marcus Afzali encouraged him to run for the seat, saying 'No, but your heart's there. You understand how the city works, through working through that development and working on your committees. That's what it takes to serve, and you've done that.'

37973.ai_summary: The City of College Park held its first in-person town hall meeting, where City Manager Kenny Young presented the city's budget for fiscal year 2024, designating a $24.8 million general fund and $11.89 million in capital improvement funding. Mayor Pro Tem Denise Mitchell explained that the city offers tax credits to developers, stating 'Yes we did phase a tax credit over five years for developers to come build in the city.' The meeting also covered concerns about student housing, with Councilmember Alan Hew suggesting the creation of an off-campus orientation guide for students moving into houses in city neighborhoods.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
Denise Mitchell, Mayor Pro Tem and Councilmember, District 4
Teresa Way-Pezzuti, Director of Human Resources
37732.ai_summary: The College Park City Council voted unanimously to raise annual compensation for the mayor and councilmembers, with the mayor's salary increasing from $10,500 to $15,000 and councilmembers' compensation going from $7,000 to $10,000. According to Assistant City Manager Bill Gardiner, 'Greenbelt, Hyattsville, Laurel and Takoma Park were considered comparable to College Park. The compensation for College Park elected officials is pretty much in the middle of these local peers.' Mayor Fazlul Kabir noted that 'Someone seeks office mainly to perform a civic duty and to support their community. However, financial compensation was always a lower-ranking criterion for running for office.'

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, Mayor of College Park
37724.ai_summary: Mayor Fazlul Kabir has been engaging with the College Park community since joining the North College Park Civic Association (NCPCA) in 2006. As he stated, 'I'm a very hyper-local person. The thing I enjoy most is talking to a neighbor on his or her porch and talking about some problems or some issues and seeing how to solve them or get things done. Just to see some kind of smile on my neighbor's face, that's the kind of thing I really enjoy.' Kabir's experience and commitment to public service have earned him the trust of the community, and he is now working to rebuild community trust in city leaders.

key_people: Erik Valentine, Community Development Planner for the City of College Park
37734.ai_summary: The City of College Park has completed an initial survey of the Sentinel Swamp Sanctuary, led by Biohabitats Inc., which identified two nontidal wetlands and recommended restoration plans, including a raised boardwalk and viewing platform. As Erik Valentine, Community Development Planner, stated, 'We're still in the very early stages of this project... We can see that there is a lot of potential there.' The city has allocated $200,000 for the project, with funding from Program Open Space, to restore the sanctuary and remove invasive species, and will continue to provide funding annually.

key_people: Violetta Sharps Jones, Vice President of the Lakeland Community Heritage Project
Julie Schablitsky, Chief of Cultural Resources with the Maryland Department of Transportation
Rev. Latishia Cokley, Pastor of Embry African Methodist Episcopal Church
37619.ai_summary: The Lakeland community gathered to unveil a historic marker, with Denise Mitchell, Mayor Pro Tem, and other state and local officials in attendance. Violetta Sharps Jones, Vice President of the Lakeland Community Heritage Project, offered a traditional ceremonial libation, stating 'We pour this libation to give honor, reverence, respect, and recognition to our ancestors whose shoulders we stand on and whose shadows we walk in.' The event celebrated the community's history and legacy, with Ed Williams expressing his love for Lakeland and the importance of passing it down to future generations, singing 'It's been a long, a long time coming, but I know a change gon' come.'

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
organizations: College Park Tree and Landscaping Board
37521.ai_summary: The City of College Park has started a sidewalk installation project on Edmonston Road, which involves cutting down a large portion of trees to build a sidewalk for mobility to bus stops and the Calvert Road Community Park. According to College Park City Councilmember John Rigg, 'The Complete Streets project is part of our broader initiatives that the city has been involved in. But it’s been about enhancing connectivity and safety for pedestrians and cyclists in our city.' However, the project has created controversy among residents, with some voicing concerns about cutting down trees, as stated by Greenbelt resident Kennedy Walker, 'The sidewalk would be useful for the students attending schools in the area walking home, but the money used to construct the sidewalk could definitely go towards other aspects of the community like more flowers and less trash in the area.'

key_people: Maria James, former managing editor of The Hyattsville Life & Times
37504.ai_summary: The College Park Here & Now hosted an election forum featuring candidates running in the May 2023 Mayoral Special Election, where they answered questions from the community, including those related to development, traffic, and affordable housing. The candidates were given a chance to respond unscripted to each question, with topics ranging from their measurable goals as mayor to their stance on non-citizen voting rights. The forum was moderated by Maria James, former managing editor of The Hyattsville Life & Times, and is available for replay.

key_people: Terry Schum, Director of the College Park Department of Planning & Community Development
Miriam Bader, Director of Planning and Development
Dhiru Thadani, Artist and Schum's husband
organizations: College Park Department of Planning & Community Development
37434.ai_summary: Terry Schum, the Director of the College Park Department of Planning & Community Development, is retiring after 37 years of service. Schum plans to rediscover her neighborhood near Rock Creek Park and pursue her lifelong interest in drawing and painting, saying 'Maybe I will carry around a sketchbook.' Her retirement marks a significant change in the city's planning and development, with Miriam Bader taking over as director on April 1.

37417.ai_summary: The City of College Park purchased a small parcel of undeveloped land in the 5100 block of Roanoke Place, in the Berwyn neighborhood, through Program Open Space, a state-funded initiative that helps local governments preserve land for recreational use. Community Development Planner Erik Valentine hopes to use the parcel as open space where people can gather and said the city “hope[s] to incorporate educational elements because of the site’s unique natural habitat” for the Sentinel Swamp Sanctuary, in Old Town. The city is seeking community input about how to move forward with the Berwyn property and will consider plans at upcoming meetings.

key_people: Maria James, Former Managing Editor and Forum Moderator
37426.ai_summary: The City of College Park is holding a mayoral election, with candidate Bryan Haddad running on a single issue to increase penalties against illegally modified vehicles. A candidate forum will be held on April 15, moderated by Maria James, where residents can submit questions for the candidates. Haddad believes that strengthening city code enforcement will have a tangible effect on the quality of the community in terms of safety and noise reduction, stating 'a vote for me is a vote for a more peaceful community'.

key_people: Patrick Wojahn, Mayor of College Park
organizations: National League of Cities University Communities Council
37262.ai_summary: College Park Mayor Patrick Wojahn resigned after being arrested on charges related to the possession and distribution of child pornography. Wojahn stated, 'While this investigation does not involve any official city business of any kind, it is in the best interests of our community that I step aside and not serve as a distraction.' Mayor Pro Tem Mitchell will serve as pro tem mayor until a special election is held within 65 days.

key_people: Robert Thurston, president of the Lakeland Civic Association
Terry Schum, director of the College Park Department of Planning & Community Development
Todd Alexander, representative of Renewal Housing Associates
organizations: College Park Department of Planning & Community Development
37038.ai_summary: The property on the corner of Route 1 and Melbourne Place in Lakeland was purchased by LV Collective for $36 million, with plans to build a student housing complex. Robert Thurston, president of the Lakeland Civic Association, emphasized the need for multigenerational housing on the adjacent Alden-Berkley site, which is undergoing upgrades. Terry Schum, director of the College Park Department of Planning & Community Development, stated that it would be ideal for the property owners to collaborate and 'listen to the community,' as part of the development review process, with Thurston noting that 'It's critical that Lakelanders and the Lakeland diaspora are involved in this redevelopment.'

key_people: Martha Ainsworth, Prince George’s Sierra Club
Kate Kennedy, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Robert Marsili, College Park Department of Public Works Director
Todd Larsen, Committee for a Better Environment (CBE) member
Alexa Bely, Committee for a Better Environment (CBE) member
organizations: College Park Department of Public Works
37031.ai_summary: The College Park City Council discussed a proposal to regulate the use of shopping bags by retail businesses, aiming to promote the city’s sustainability goals by reducing shoppers’ reliance on single-use bags. The proposed ordinance would ban plastic carryout bags and require stores to charge customers a minimum $0.10 fee for paper or new reusable bags. Councilmember John Rigg expressed support for the bill and requested that the city’s implementation include distribution of reusable bags in targeted locations to ensure support for people who may struggle to afford bag fees.

key_people: Rebecca Bailey, College Park Animal Control Officer
Bob Ryan, College Park Department of Public Services Director
organizations: College Park Department of Public Services
37024.ai_summary: College Park Animal Control Officer Rebecca Bailey loves her job because it gives her the opportunity to help animals, saying 'They don’t have a way to advocate for themselves.' Bailey has a wide range of responsibilities, including picking up stray pets and educating the public about animal care. The city has a small animal shelter where it temporarily houses rescued pets, and Bailey works to care for these animals and reunite them with their owners, with pets that are not reunited possibly being put up for adoption, as seen with residents Aron Bender and Bryan Draper who adopted pets from the shelter.

key_people: Anna Latta Owens, College Park Mayor
William E. 'Brit' Kirwan, University of Maryland Leader
key_issues: independent living facility for seniors and persons with disabilities
36789.ai_summary: Anna Latta Owens, College Park's first and only female mayor, played a significant role in the city's governance from 1981 to 1993. She led a fight against a proposed garbage incinerator and established strong, positive ties with the University of Maryland, then under the leadership of William E. 'Brit' Kirwan. Anna's legacy continues to be remembered fondly by those who knew her, as she touched many lives improving them with her measures, including her work on the Attick Towers independent living facility.

36796.ai_summary: The City of College Park has introduced a new program to make composting easier for residents, providing inexpensive compost containers and free weekly collections. As stated in the article, 'Composting is one of the easiest and most productive things individuals can do for the environment' and can help combat climate change by reducing the amount of methane produced in landfills. The program allows residents to compost a wide range of food scraps and other materials, making it a significant step towards environmental sustainability in College Park.

36528.ai_summary: The College Park City Council unanimously approved a new district map, the October Plan 1 map, which will be in effect prior to the November 2023 city election. Councilmembers supported this map as it keeps the core neighborhoods intact, unlike the Plan 3A map which would have unevenly distributed the city's civic associations, as noted by Councilmember Susan Whitney, who said 'The fact that so many civic associations would be concentrated in District 2 really shows an imbalance.' Councilmember Maria Mackie also noted that 'One of the reasons that Autoville and Cherry Hill will no longer be in District 4 is because they’re the means to allowing District 1 to come down into [areas with student housing].'

key_people: Tyrone Wells, Operations Manager of the Prince George’s County Office of Emergency Management
organizations: Prince George’s County Office of Emergency Management
key_issues: support for seniors and homebound individuals
36357.ai_summary: Tyrone Wells, the operations manager of the Prince George’s County Office of Emergency Management, presented the updated County Emergency Operations Plan to the College Park City Council. Councilmembers expressed concerns about the effectiveness of the county's emergency notification system, with Councilmember John Rigg stating 'Citizens in my community are wondering why they don’t hear from the county more often.' The council also discussed the need for better support for seniors and homebound individuals during emergencies, with Councilmember Maria Mackie noting 'A lot of our senior citizens don’t even own a phone.'

key_people: Gale Mamatova, Vice President of the North College Park Community Association
36359.ai_summary: The City of College Park is undergoing redistricting, a process that has been a heated discussion among residents. Gale Mamatova, Vice President of the North College Park Community Association, suggests that the city needs to revisit its approach to bringing residents along through access to information and effective communication, and invest in a community engagement tool to facilitate this process. Mamatova emphasizes the importance of listening with intent, stating that 'those in leadership positions with the City of College Park need to invest in learning to listen with intent so that people around them feel more represented, supported and understood'.

key_people: Kathy Bryant, President of the Old Town Civic Association and District 3 resident
Jordan Dewar, Vice-Chair of the Redistricting Commission
Robert Day, Chair of the Redistricting Commission
36067.ai_summary: The College Park City Council's redistricting debate continued with residents and councilmembers questioning the Redistricting Commission about their proposals, which would separate the four core District 3 neighborhoods. Kathy Bryant, President of the Old Town Civic Association, said '[The commission] kept saying ‘we follow the numbers,’ but it’s disregarding long term neighborhood connections that I feel are very important.' Councilmember Fazlul Kabir stated that 'people don’t really differentiate between the boundaries of these neighborhoods; they all think as one cohesive community,' and emphasized the importance of keeping the district intact.

36180.ai_summary: College Park Day, the city's signature celebration, is returning on Oct. 15 after a 2-year hiatus, with about 150 organizations participating, including food vendors, live entertainment, and more. Councilmember Denise Mitchell recalled that College Park Day grew out of discussions among Councilmember Fazlul Kabir, Mayor Patrick Wojahn, and a small group of residents. Band leader Shawn Sharifi said, 'We can't wait to bring our boogy to College Park Day,' and around 8,000 residents are expected to attend the event, which will feature a variety of local food vendors and entertainment, including Gymkana, a gymnastic and acrobatics troupe at the University of Maryland.

key_people: Lisbeth M Melendez Rivera, Ward 2 Council Candidate
Robert Croslin, Mayor of College Park
Kevin Ward, Former Mayor of College Park
35987.ai_summary: Emily Strab has won the Ward 2 city council election with 280 votes, edging out Lisbeth M Melendez Rivera and Kelly Burello. The Ward 2 seat was vacant after former councilmember Robert Croslin was elected mayor in a June special mayoral election following former mayor Kevin Ward's death in January. Strab will take the oath of office on October 20 at King Park.

key_people: Lee Sommer, College Park Airport Manager
David Dorsch, Chair of the College Park Airport Authority
organizations: Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission
35792.ai_summary: The College Park City Council discussed a new commuter program run by Tailwind Air, which would operate out of the College Park Airport with four daily flights between College Park and New York City’s Skyport Marina. According to David Dorsch, Chair of the College Park Airport Authority, 'The noise is generally the helicopters and the ones that are the worst are the military, because they’re the biggest and heaviest,' and the planes in Tailwind’s fleet are quieter than those aircraft. The airport would need to change its operating regulations to allow Tailwind to maintain their proposed flight schedules, and the council was due to revisit this issue during their Sept. 13 worksession.

key_people: Christopher Hatcher, Attorney for Perseus TDC
35965.ai_summary: A new apartment building, called Residences at the Six, is under construction by Perseus TDC at 6400 America Boulevard in the Prince George’s Plaza Transit District. Councilmember Ben Simasek said that the new building “is a big opportunity to activate University Town Center, to improve the walkability of that area, and generally making it more attractive.” The developer has committed to designate at least 75% of the units as affordable to low- and middle-income households, with three-quarters of those apartments having rents under $2,500 initially.

key_people: Kathy Bryant, fourth generation College Park homeowner
35659.ai_summary: A devastating bow echo storm hit College Park on July 12, causing widespread damage and power outages. According to Assistant City Manager Bill Gardiner, 'The department of public works estimates that several hundred trees came down during the storm,' and the cost to the city is estimated to be around $750,000. Resident Victoria MacDonald said, 'Maybe that has to be looked at, in my opinion, what are the criteria?' regarding the University of Maryland's use of its warning siren to caution residents of the impending storm.

key_people: Calvin Hawkins, Prince George’s County Council Member
Mel Franklin, Prince George’s County Council Member
35657.ai_summary: The primary election in Prince George’s County saw a voter turnout of nearly 214,000, with incumbents Calvin Hawkins and Mel Franklin winning the at-large race. However, some residents, including Jennifer Kubit, reported issues with the voting process, such as being allowed to choose between Republican and Democratic ballots despite being registered with a specific party. The College Park City Council discussed staffing issues that stalled the opening of the Ritchie Coliseum polling site, inconveniencing voters who had arrived on time, as Kubit said, her spouse 'accidentally selected the wrong ballot' but was able to void it and submit a correct one.

key_people: James Crane, communications and public safety expert with the Columbia Telecommunications Corporation
Shawn Thompson, vice president for analytics with CTC
Suellen Ferguson, attorney for the city
35636.ai_summary: The College Park City Council discussed the planned installation of 5G antennas around the city with representatives from the Columbia Telecommunications Corporation. Residents expressed concerns about health risks to those living and traveling in close proximity to the proposed Small Wireless Facilities, citing a 2020 article in the peer-reviewed medical journal Oncology Letters that noted the evaluation of radio-frequency radiation health risks from 5G technology had not been properly reviewed. According to Suellen Ferguson, attorney for the city, a 2018 FCC rule requires that jurisdictions approve eligible Small Wireless Facility installation requests within 60 days, and the city can only discuss the exact location of proposed arrays with telecommunications enterprises, but not whether arrays can be installed at all.

key_people: Lisbeth M. Melendez Rivera, City Council Candidate
35721.ai_summary: A staffing issue led to a polling place in College Park opening an hour and a half late. College Park is mentioned in the context of a broader elections roundup, which includes a special mayoral election in Hyattsville won by Robert Croslin with 1,329 votes. Upcoming elections include a special election for a Hyattsville city council seat and the general election on November 8.

key_people: Mel Franklin, Incumbent At-Large County Councilmember
Calvin Hawkins, Incumbent At-Large County Councilmember
Paul Pinsky, Incumbent Maryland State Senator
Alonzo T. Washington, Incumbent State Delegate
35522.ai_summary: The unofficial results are in from the July 19 primary election, with many races essentially decided in the Democratic primaries. Wanika Fisher is leading in the county council District 2 election, while Eric Olson has secured his nomination in District 3. Angela Alsobrooks has won the county executive election with 91% of the votes. The results are current as of the morning of July 20th, but could still change as not all precincts have finished reporting and mail-in and provisional ballots have not yet been counted. The elections will impact College Park governance as the city is part of county council District 3 and has a small part in District 1.

key_people: James Rosapepe, State Senator and Vice Chair of the State Senate’s Budget and Taxation Committee
35361.ai_summary: The City of College Park is receiving over $22 million in funding from the State of Maryland for various projects, including $9.7 million for the Route 1 rebuilding project and $5 million for graduate student housing at the University of Maryland. State Senator James Rosapepe stated, “I call it the Champs-Élysées of College Park” and noted that the improvements will increase safety and create a visually pleasing thoroughfare. The funding will also support the creation of the Community Preservation Trust to preserve affordable single-family houses in the city, with Rosapepe explaining, “Our notion is that over time, we want to get more state money to subsidize more graduate student housing.”

key_people: Rudy Anthony, At-Large County Council Candidate
Leo Eyombo, At-Large County Council Candidate
Sam Elira, At-Large County Council Candidate
Mel Franklin, At-Large County Council Candidate
Stanford Fraser, At-Large County Council Candidate
Calvin Hawkins, At-Large County Council Candidate
Jonathan White, At-Large County Council Candidate
John D.B. Carr, Prince George’s County Sheriff Candidate
Dave Grogan, Prince George’s County Sheriff Candidate
Elliott Gibson, Prince George’s County Sheriff Candidate
Sylvester Jones, Prince George’s County Sheriff Candidate
Loralyn Mayo, Prince George’s County Sheriff Candidate
Yakeisha Dawn Hines-Poole, Former Prince George’s County Sheriff Candidate
Malcolm Thomas Colombo, Former State Delegate Candidate
35345.ai_summary: The state primary election is scheduled to take place on July 19, with early voting from July 7 to July 14. Candidates such as Eric Olson, Sia Finoh, and Eve Shuman are running for District 3, while Tom Dernoga is running unopposed in District 1. The election matters for College Park governance as the outcome will determine the representatives for the area in the county council and state legislature. As noted, 'Maryland voters must register with a particular party to vote in a partisan primary.'

key_people: Claire Worshtil, lead strategic park planner
Mary Cook, former NCPCA president and former College Park city councilmember
organizations: Prince George’s County Department of Parks and Recreation
35359.ai_summary: The Prince George’s County Department of Parks and Recreation is planning a new community center in North College Park, with a feasibility study expected to conclude by the end of 2022. As Claire Worshtil, lead strategic park planner, noted, 'We can create flexible spaces,' such as having 'seniors could use a room in the mornings, and teens could use the same room … in the afternoons.' The project has received $1 million in support from the Maryland state budget, secured by State Delegate Ben Barnes, and the City of College Park has also pledged financial support, aiming to bring the community together and promote inclusion, as incoming NCPCA President Alaina Pitt said, 'I think the community center can serve as a catalyst for bringing the community together and for more interaction between neighbors.'

key_people: Mel Franklin, At-Large County Council Member
Calvin Hawkins, At-Large County Council Member
34640.ai_summary: The state primary election in Maryland is scheduled for July 19, with early voting from July 7 to July 14. In Prince George's County, several candidates are running for county council seats, including Wanika Fisher, Victor Ramirez, and Eric Olson. The election is significant for College Park governance as most of the city is located in county council District 3, and the outcome will impact local representation and decision-making.

35179.ai_summary: The College Park City Council passed a law governing tree pruning and removal, requiring residents to obtain a permit to cut down trees over 36 inches in circumference and to prune more than 20% of live branches on large trees. Resident Robert O'Brien noted that trees are a community resource and it took generations for neighborhoods to recover their tree canopy after diseased trees were removed in Chicago during his childhood. The new law has been controversial, with some residents believing homeowners should be able to make decisions about trees on their own property without city oversight, while others think tree canopy is a public good that the city should regulate to preserve.

key_people: Jeff DeHan, Associate Director of Stormwater Management
organizations: Prince George's County Stormwater Management Division
Prince George's County Department of the Environment
Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission
35203.ai_summary: The city of College Park is addressing stormwater management issues in the Calvert Hills neighborhood, which has been plagued by flooding due to aging and inadequate infrastructure. As Councilmember John Rigg noted, 'During the massive rainstorm of September 2020, there was literally 3 to 4 feet of water in the streets. And some neighbors experience flooded basements or sewer back-ups nearly every year.' The Prince George's County Stormwater Management Division has proposed a four-part improvement plan, estimated to cost $15.5 million, which includes widening and deepening the open channel and increasing the capacity of underground culverts to handle more frequent storms.

key_people: Mike Hunninghake, Program Manager of Sustainable Maryland
organizations: University of Maryland’s Environmental Finance Center
35210.ai_summary: Sustainable Maryland's free certification program helps municipalities become more eco-friendly, with 41 certified municipalities in the state, including College Park. As Mike Hunninghake, Program Manager of Sustainable Maryland, stated, 'The program operates on the premise that by engaging local governments in sustainability we build a strong foundation for the work that needs to be done with respect to critical issues like climate change, environmental justice, and strong local economies.' College Park has established its own Green Team, the Committee for a Better Environment, to advise the city council on existing environmental issues and implement corresponding sustainability programs.

key_people: Carlo Colella, University of Maryland Vice President and Chief Administrative Officer
35042.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has reinstated funding for live-video monitoring capacity in the fiscal 2023 budget, with the University of Maryland Police Department (UMPD) to monitor 22 cameras in the city. According to UMPD Chief David Mitchell, 'One of our major concerns is the increased call for service as well as mental health,' and live-monitoring can assist with a more proactive response to safety issues. The program, which costs $592 per camera per month, will be monitored by UMD students employed by the Security Operations Center, alongside permanent staff members, as noted by Councilmember Llatera Brown Esters, who said 'Live-monitoring, in addition to police patrols and other safety precautions, can assist with a more proactive response to criminal and safety issues that arise.'

key_people: Robert Marsili, Director of Public Works
Bill Lescure, descendant of the Calverts
Thomas Taltavull, Principal with TJT Architects
organizations: Maryland National-Capital Park and Planning Commission
35026.ai_summary: The City of College Park has been restoring the historic Old Parish House, a single-story structure built in 1817, with the goal of making it a 'showpiece for the public', according to Robert Marsili, the city's director of public works. The restoration, which has been ongoing for several years, has involved replacing the roof and ceilings, repairing and replacing windows, and exterior masonry work, with a focus on historical accuracy. 'We worked hard to do this in a historically accurate way,' Marsili said, noting that the city chose to use a local firm for the project.

key_people: Petrella Robinson, Mayor of North Brentwood and Commission Member
Isabella Alcañiz, Associate Professor and Director of the University of Maryland’s Latin American and Caribbean Studies Center and Commission Member
35037.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has established a Restorative Justice Commission to address the historical harm caused by the city's urban renewal project in the 1970s, which destroyed many homes in the Lakeland community. Mayor Patrick Wojahn stated that the initiative aims to “make the community whole for what occurred in the past and put them as close as possible into the place or even better than they were before the wrong occurred.” The commission, which includes members such as Petrella Robinson and Isabella Alcañiz, will work to restore and preserve the Lakeland community, with Robinson noting that the community “need[s] restitution because they, they put them out of their homes, they came in and did what they wanted to do. It was a great, and is a great community”.

key_people: Hayden Renaghan, UMD Student Government Association’s (SGA) deputy liaison to the College Park City Council
Kislay Parahsar, UMD student body president
Dan Alpert, former SGA liaison to the city council
organizations: College Park Department of Public Works
35015.ai_summary: Hayden Renaghan, the UMD Student Government Association’s deputy liaison to the College Park City Council, is working to make a positive impact in the city through the Clean City Project, a collaborative initiative that tackles weekly street clean-ups. As Renaghan explained, 'Students want it known that it’s their town, too, not just a pit-stop for them.' Renaghan's leadership and dedication to the project have been recognized by her peers, including UMD student body president Kislay Parahsar, who noted that she 'continues to demonstrate a passionate commitment to the projects she works on in her new role as chief of staff'.

key_people: Mary Cook, President of the North College Park Citizens Association
Claire Worshtil, Lead Strategic Park Planner for M-NCPPC
organizations: Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission (M-NCPPC)
locations: College Park Community Center-Youth Sports Complex
34892.ai_summary: The city of College Park has initiated a feasibility study for a new community center in the North College Park neighborhood, with the Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission (M-NCPPC) expected to cover most of the costs. As Councilmember Fazul Kabir (District 1) stated, 'A partnership will help expedite the construction. This will also help the city to have its stake in the way the services will be provided in the center once it’s built.' The study, led by WXY Studio, will assess community needs and priorities, ensuring that the new center complements existing services, including the planned multigenerational center near the Mall at Prince Georges, as noted by Claire Worshtil, lead strategic park planner for M-NCPPC, who said 'We want to make sure that we are complementing services, not duplicating them.'

34748.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is focused on encouraging homeownership through new programs, aiming to achieve a better balance of rental and owner-occupied homes in the city. According to Councilmember Fazlul Kabir, 'It’s not against the renters. It’s just the added benefit of stabilizing the neighborhood,' and Whitney noted that 'Homeowners simply have more skin in the game than renters, who’d have the option to move, were the city to decline.' The council is considering adjustments to the existing New Neighbor Homeowner Grant Program, which would offer potential home buyers $5,000 to offset closing costs if they agree to stay in their home for at least five years.

34737.ai_summary: The College Park City Council approved a contract to purchase a property in the Old Town neighborhood for $1.7 million, with the goal of stabilizing affordable rentals and exploring community-focused development. Councilmember Stuart Adams noted that 'The potential purchase of 4704 Calvert Road makes sense... It aligns with our strategic goals.' However, some council members and residents expressed concerns about the speed of the process, with Councilmember Fazlul Kabir stating 'I will be supporting this, but I am very uncomfortable with how fast this is moving.' Tenant D.W. Rowlands also voiced concerns, saying 'While I often hear anti-renter and anti-rental-property rhetoric from residents and elected officials in the city, I hope that the mayor and council will keep in mind that renters are an important part of the community.'

key_people: Bibi Mohamed, Director of Helpers to Good
Azad Mohamed, Volunteer with Helpers to Good
34755.ai_summary: Helpers to Good, a program under Ansaar-ul-Birr Community Services Inc., hosted a Kids Fun Festival on Feb. 19 and Feb. 20, giving free lunches and school supplies to kids. The event was supported by the city and a national relief organization, with Bibi Mohamed noting that 'The lunch was sponsored by a grant from the city of College Park, and the gift was from one of our [partners], Islamic Relief [U.S.A]'. The festival's success is a testament to the importance of community connections, with Helpers to Good actively supporting the local community with programs designed to serve students, seniors, refugees, and others in need.

key_people: John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Sarah Christopherson, President of the school’s PTSO
34718.ai_summary: Hyattsville Middle School students are facing delayed bus arrivals, with up to 75% of buses arriving late in February, which is affecting their education. As John Rigg, a College Park City Councilmember, notes, "My own child’s bus late arrival means that he misses math pretty much daily." School Board Member Pamela Boozer-Strother is advocating for improved bus services, stressing that busing HMS students should be a priority due to the disruptions caused by the school's construction. The issue may be contributing to a decreased attendance rate and declining enrollment at the school.

key_people: Denise Mitchell, College Park City Councilmember
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
Kate Kennedy, College Park City Councilmember
Llatetra Brown Esters, College Park City Councilmember
34743.ai_summary: College Park City Councilmembers have been navigating changes in meeting locations and formats over the past two years, including moving from the old city hall building to Davis Hall and then to virtual meetings due to the pandemic. Councilmembers such as Denise Mitchell and Maria Mackie noted that meeting in Davis Hall was 'cramped' and preferred the old city hall, but also acknowledged the benefits of virtual meetings, including improved work-life balance, as stated by Kate Kennedy: 'It's a lot easier to have work-life balance when you're at home.' Llatetra Brown Esters also noted that virtual meetings allowed for greater participation from constituents, stating 'I think that the virtual option has allowed more people to participate, which I think is important.'

key_people: Michael Williams, College Park Economic Development Manager
organizations: Prince George’s County Economic Development Corporation
34775.ai_summary: The College Park City Council discussed a proposal to renew the city’s participation in the Greater College Park Regional Institution Strategic Enterprise (RISE) Zone. According to College Park Economic Development Manager Michael Williams, “What we think is that start up companies and new companies are generally trying to get going and not trying to buy or purchase a building,” and the state has expanded the RISE zone program to provide rental assistance to businesses that don’t own their own buildings. The city will hold a public hearing to review participation in the RISE zone at 7:30 p.m. on March 8.

key_people: Darryll Pines, University of Maryland President
Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember (District 3)
Julie Bringman, Pastor of Hope Lutheran Church
organizations: Maryland-National Capital Park and Planning Commission (M-NCPPC)
34586.ai_summary: The Adelphi Road Sector Plan, which aims to guide development around the Adelphi Road station for the next 25 years, has met with opposition from residents who are concerned about the upzoning of Guilford Woods, a 15-acre state-owned tract with mature trees and wildlife. Delegate Mary Lehman and College Park City Councilmember Stuart Adams have spoken out against the plan, with Adams urging the county council to delay moving forward, saying 'We have enough time to really thoughtfully consider how to add appropriate density, how to make sure we’re looking at our school burdens, how we can preserve our green space and make this absolutely fantastic.' The College Park City Council has requested that the Prince George’s County Council and the county planning board delay moving the sector plan forward.

34607.ai_summary: The City of College Park is aiming to upgrade its stormwater management systems, particularly in the Calvert Hills neighborhood which has been severely affected by flooding. The city plans to conduct a topographical survey to examine water flow in the area, with an estimated cost of $600,000, and Councilmember Stuart Adams expects the study to take about a year to complete. As College Park resident Bob Baer noted, 'When Calvert road stormwater inlets max out, and the street water level rises only an inch or more, the street water goes down our driveway and into our yard and the neighbor’s yard.'

key_people: Robert Craig Baum, Wisdom 1096 project creator
34475.ai_summary: The Here & Now has reflected on the pandemic with residents, including Maxine Gross, who shared her personal experience of being busy with virtual activities during lockdown. She noted that the city passed Resolution 20-R016 in support of Black lives in June 2020, which included an apology for the city's past history of oppression and a commitment to aggressively seek opportunities for restorative justice. The city later adopted a new resolution establishing a restorative justice commission, as residents continued to navigate personal challenges and reflect on the best and worst of times during the pandemic.

key_people: Stuart Adams, College Park City Councilmember
Robert Day, Former College Park City Councilmember
34307.ai_summary: Stuart Adams was elected to represent District 3 on the College Park City Council, replacing Robert Day. Adams has been active in the community since 2015 and plans to use his expertise in floodplain management and sustainability to tackle stormwater management issues in District 3. He said, 'I’ve always been drawn to university towns. I like the activity and being able to meet younger folks,' and 'That’s been my passion for a while, community resilience. I know what it’s like when your family loses your home and your community gets completely uprooted,' referencing his family's experience with Hurricane Katrina.

34310.ai_summary: Susan Whitney is the new District 2 representative on the College Park City Council, replacing Monroe Dennis. Whitney, who has worked in various fields and has been involved in her community through volunteering, aims to foster more community involvement in her district. As she takes her seat on the council, Whitney said, 'I love solving problems, getting things done and helping people. I want to be persistent and see what we can do to make positive change for our neighbors.'

key_people: Calvin Hawkins, At-Large Council Member and Chair
Deni Taveras, District 2 Council Member and Vice Chair
Eric Olson, College Park Resident and Former Council Member
34197.ai_summary: The Prince George's County Council voted to accept a new electoral map, despite widespread citizen opposition, which divides the city of College Park and makes some potential candidates ineligible to run for office. As College Park Mayor Patrick Wojahn said, 'The current proposed map divides College Park right through its center.' Deni Taveras, the vice chair of the council, defended the map, stating 'This map does more for black and brown communities than any other map ever submitted.' The new map's impact on College Park governance and upcoming elections has sparked controversy and disappointment among some council members, including Dannielle Glaros, who expressed her disappointment with the map, saying 'I deeply question the moral compass of my colleagues.'

key_people: Mel Franklin, Prince George’s County Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Eric Olson, District 3 county council candidate
33953.ai_summary: Prince George’s County Councilmember Mel Franklin proposed a redistricting plan that redesignates portions of College Park from District 3 to District 1, while keeping the University of Maryland in District 3. This plan was passed 6 to 4 in a council meeting on Oct. 19, after an earlier proposal by Councilmember Derrick Leon Davis was met with public outrage, with Davis' map placing the entirety of College Park in District 1. As Councilmember Thomas Dernoga said, 'With regard to the City of College Park, they’re blindsided,' highlighting concerns about transparency in the redistricting process.

organizations: College Park Restorative Justice Steering Committee
34106.ai_summary: The College Park Restorative Justice Steering Committee presented their suggestions to the city council, aiming to establish a Restorative Justice Commission to address harms done to the Lakeland community. As Mayor Patrick Wojahn said, 'There are a lot of folks in the College Park community that may not be aware of this history,' and 'We're doing what we can to get the word out, to get a better understanding of what happened with the urban renewal process.' The committee's report included recommendations for commission activities over a potential six-year timeline, including seeking and facilitating community engagement and holding truth-telling events.

organizations: Town of University Park Common Council
34137.ai_summary: The College Park City Council debated a ban on gas-powered leaf blowers, sponsored by Danny Schaible, with the goal of reducing noise and gas pollution in the city. Schaible stated that using a gas-powered blower for an hour is equivalent to driving a Ford F-150 for 3,000 miles, and that it can be as loud as 110 decibels, saying '[A gas-powered leaf blower] can be as loud as 110 decibels, 115 decibels in the ear of an operator.' The proposed ban aims to preserve the local environment and reduce health risks, with neighboring jurisdictions such as University Park and Montgomery County also considering similar bans.

key_people: Ken Ulman, President of Margrave Strategies
Stacey Price, Co-founder and CEO of Shop Made in D.C.
Michael Babin, Co-founder of Shop Made in D.C.
