key_issues: Support for Local Artists and Makers
34108.ai_summary: The College Park City Council has approved the use of funds from the American Rescue Plan Act of 2021 to support the opening of Shop Made in Maryland, a retail store featuring products from local artisans, in the new College Park City Hall. According to Stacey Price, Co-founder and CEO of Shop Made in D.C., 'We like to label ourselves as a modern retail incubator. We provide a place to showcase talent … We provide business assistance so they can become more profitable in what they are doing, from packaging to pricing and all the other things.' The store aims to support artists and makers by giving them space to show their work and the opportunity to build a customer base. Ken Ulman, President of Margrave Strategies, noted that 'The retail world is not an easy world to find unique retailers. Our team knew we needed to find something that captured and reflected our shared values, and demonstrated an exciting vision of the City of College Park.'

key_people: Lisa Walker, Chair of Hyattsville Aging in Place
Miriam Bader, College Park's Senior City Planner
Agnes Artemel, President of Artemel & Associates Inc.
organizations: University of Baltimore's Jacob France Institute
34100.ai_summary: The City of College Park is working to become a more age-friendly community by applying to join AARP's Network of Age-Friendly States and Communities. As part of this effort, a team of consultants is developing an action plan to address issues of aging in the community, with a focus on areas such as transportation, housing, and social activities. According to Miriam Bader, College Park's senior city planner, 'When you can no longer drive, then it is difficult to age in place, due to lack of walkable grocery stores and walkable medical facilities.'

33771.ai_summary: The College Park Here and Now hosted a virtual forum for candidates for Mayor as well as Districts 1, 2, and 4. Questions were made from the College Park Here and Now reporting, as well as from local residents. The forums allowed residents to hear directly from their representatives before the upcoming election, featuring candidates such as Patrick Wojahn for Mayor, and Kate Kennedy and Fazlul Kabir for District 1.

key_people: Mary Cook, President of the North College Park Neighborhood Association
33836.ai_summary: The Countywide Map Amendment (CMA) process has sparked concerns among College Park legislators and community activists, who fear that the new zoning classifications will substantively change the character of their neighborhoods. College Park Mayor Patrick Wojahn sent a letter to the county council expressing concerns that the city council and staff did not have more input into the zoning decisions, and Mary Cook, president of the North College Park Neighborhood Association, stated 'I am concerned that these new zoning classifications are going to set developers up so that they can create a new density in our area.' The CMA process is a non-substantive, technical exercise, but activists like Danny Schaible are concerned that it may favor developers and create loopholes for zoning intensifications.

33888.ai_summary: Several construction projects are underway in College Park, including the Tempo student housing project, the University of Maryland's new School of Public Policy building, and a new City Hall. These projects are expected to be completed in summer 2022, with the City Hall moving its operations by the end of the calendar year. The upgrades to Baltimore Avenue aim to transform it into a college town main street, creating a safer environment for pedestrians, cyclists, and motorists.

key_people: Karen Stewart, Owner of Jesse's Soul Line Dance
Melissa Sites, Director of College Park Arts Exchange
Rev. Carrington Carter, Head Pastor at Embry AME
33883.ai_summary: The Embry AME Church in College Park's historically Black neighborhood of Lakeland hosted a dance event, where members and other city residents danced to religiously themed remixes of popular songs. As Rev. Carrington Carter, the head pastor at Embry AME, reflected on the history of the church, he said, 'I'm very informed about the restorative justice act ... the church has weathered a lot of storms as far as its history.' The event is part of the church's efforts to move forward and make a positive impact in the community, following the City of College Park's resolution renouncing systemic racism and declaring support for Black lives.

key_people: Fazlul Kabir, College Park City Councilmember
John Rigg, College Park City Councilmember
Alex Keating, Director of Public Policy and Partnerships at VeoRide
Robert Day, College Park City Councilmember
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
33773.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is debating the extension of VeoRide's micro-mobility pilot program, despite concerns about mismanagement and improperly parked vehicles. Councilmembers, including John Rigg and Robert Day, have expressed reservations about the program, with Rigg stating 'I've seen them throw them in the stream, I've seen them be hit with cars, I've seen people throw them in the streets,' and Day saying 'These things are not being put back, I know they are a great addition to our city, but if we can't manage them right then we can't have them here.' The council is seeking accountability from VeoRide and discussing the potential impact on the city and its residents.

key_people: Stacy Hornstein, Director of Development for Republic Properties
Terry Schum, College Park Director of Planning, Community and Economic Development
Mark Vogel, Founder of Mark Vogel Companies
key_issues: hotel industry impact from COVID-19 pandemic
33651.ai_summary: Republic Properties, the developer of a site in College Park, is seeking to change the zoning to build a multifamily development instead of a hotel due to the COVID-19 pandemic's severe impact on the hospitality industry. As Stacy Hornstein, Director of Development for Republic Properties, stated, 'The financing community who would participate with us … has evaporated. You can’t get financing for a hotel today.' The proposed development, which would have smaller units marketed to young professionals and the 55-and-older community, has received support from Councilmember Denise Mitchell, and its proximity to the College Park Metro and Purple Line stations ensures easy access to the University of Maryland campus.

key_people: Todd Reitzel, Member of the Tree and Landscape Board
Dr. Ian Mather, College Park Resident
33577.ai_summary: The College Park City Council delayed a vote on a proposed tree ordinance after a public hearing where around 30 residents testified, with many arguing that the ordinance would put an undue burden on homeowners to maintain the tree canopy. As Emily Friend said, 'I believe if we’re going to have a successful tree planting program, it should not be punitive,' and she worries that fines for cutting down trees without replanting new ones may lead residents to 'choose a fine and then be very bitter about it and never want to plant a tree again.' The ordinance aims to boost the city's waning tree canopy, with residents needing a free permit to cut down a tree on their property or prune one more than 20%.

key_people: Jax Chaudhry, Literacy Lab Regional Director
33567.ai_summary: The College Park City Council is partnering with The Literacy Lab, a nonprofit organization, to provide reading tutors for the 2021-2022 school year at Hollywood Elementary School. Councilmember Maria Mackie said, 'We saw a problem. We found a solution in the Literacy Lab and we worked on implementing that solution in hopes that this small step could be the first step to helping many students throughout College Park and PG County.' The program will account for no more than $40,000 out of College Park’s FY22 budget, and aims to address the literacy gap among young students, particularly K-3 students, as stated by Literacy Lab Regional Director Jax Chaudhry, 'Our mission at the Literacy Lab is to provide children with individualized reading instruction to close the literacy gap.'

key_people: Major James Keleti, commander of PGPD’s Special Operations Division
Bob Ryan, College Park Public Services Director
Captain Gilbert Bowling, PGPD’s assistant commander
33505.ai_summary: The College Park City Council and Prince George’s County Police Department are exploring enforcement options in response to an increase in noise complaints from noisy cars. Major James Keleti confirmed that the number of noise complaints has ticked up, and police have issued 26 warnings and 29 equipment repair orders for excessive exhaust noise. Councilmember Fazlul Kabir said, 'This issue is getting worse,' and emphasized the need for additional enforcement options, citing a joint operation in Montgomery County as a potential model.

key_people: Robert Marsili, director of College Park’s Department of Public Works
organizations: College Park Department of Public Works
33469.ai_summary: The City of College Park's Department of Public Works director, Robert Marsili, explained that every city or town in Prince George’s County handles its solid waste differently. He noted that “Every city or town in Prince George’s County handles its solid waste a little differently” and that local jurisdictions evolved different policies over time. The county has expanded and upgraded its facilities, such as the Brown Station Road Sanitary Landfill, to meet EPA requirements and improve waste management practices.

key_people: Pablo Regis de Oliveira, Co-founder and Executive Director of EducArte
Pablo Regis de Oliveira, Program Manager for the Prince George’s County Arts and Humanities Council
Melissa Sites, Executive Director of College Park Arts Exchange
Patrick Wojahn, Mayor of College Park
André Coelho, Percussionist and Pandeiro Player
organizations: Prince George’s County Arts and Humanities Council
33507.ai_summary: The College Park Arts Exchange, in collaboration with EducArte, hosted a Brazilian percussion workshop at the Old Parish House, led by percussionist André Coelho. Pablo Regis de Oliveira, co-founder and executive director of EducArte, organized the workshops, stating 'I’ve been driving by this building for so many years, and it’s nice to actually be doing an event here that’s for our community.' The event was part of the city's efforts to resume in-person events as pandemic-related restrictions were lifted, with Mayor Patrick Wojahn and the College Park City Council supporting the initiative.

key_people: Patrick Wojahn, Mayor of College Park
Fazlul Kabir, College Park City Councilmember
Maria Mackie, College Park City Councilmember
key_issues: Grant assistance for small businesses and nonprofits
30398.ai_summary: College Park is expecting $22 million from the American Rescue Plan Act by the end of July, with the first half of the funds scheduled to arrive soon. Mayor Patrick Wojahn and the city council discussed how to use the incoming funds, with suggestions including paying off the city's financial losses and providing grant assistance to hotels, restaurants, and nonprofits. As Councilmember Fazlul Kabir noted, 'This is a huge opportunity for us to invest... to recover from what we have lost due to the COVID pandemic.'
